proposals.title: Scaling the Robustness-Fluency Trade-off: A Systematic Study of Generative LLMs Under NLP Distribution Shifts
proposals.motivation: Prior work on smaller, discriminative models has shown a strong correlation between in-distribution performance and out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness. However, it is unknown if this correlation holds for modern, generative large language models (LLMs) which are trained on massive, multi-source datasets and optimized for instruction-following. Understanding this relationship is critical for predicting and improving the real-world reliability of LLMs as they are deployed in diverse, shifting environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strong in-distribution to out-of-distribution correlation observed in smaller models breaks down for generative LLMs, as their scale and instruction-tuning prioritize fluency and generalization in ways that may decouple standard accuracy from robustness to specific distribution shifts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scale study, evaluating a suite of pre-trained and instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., LLaMA, T5, and GPT-3 series variants) on a curated 'multi-dataset' of NLP tasks with established distribution shifts (e.g., HANS for NLI, PAWS for paraphrase, and adversarial QA splits). We will measure both standard accuracy and a robustness metric (relative performance drop) for each model. The core analysis will involve correlating these metrics across model scales and training stages, using the statistical framework from Taori et al.'s prior work on 'hallucination' and prompting for comprehension.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence on how scaling and instruction-tuning affect robustness correlations, offering a more nuanced understanding of LLM reliability. The findings will guide the development of more robust training and evaluation protocols, moving beyond in-distribution benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While distribution shift studies exist, none have systematically tested the core robustness correlation hypothesis on the scale and architecture of modern generative LLMs, nor examined the specific impact of instruction-tuning, a dominant pre-2026 alignment method.

proposals.title: Steering Foundation Models via Closed-Loop Dataset Evolution with Learned Critic Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current methods for aligning foundation models, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) or direct fine-tuning, are often static, costly to update, and can lead to performance trade-offs. There is a critical need for more adaptive, data-centric steering mechanisms that can continuously correct model deficiencies—like hallucination or safety violations—identified post-deployment. This proposal addresses the core challenge of how to translate high-level behavioral critiques into precise, scalable interventions on a model's training data distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned critic model, trained to audit a foundation model's outputs against a multifaceted specification, can provide failure analyses that, when algorithmically inverted, can effectively guide a data curation engine to evolve the training dataset. This closed-loop process will enable systematic and targeted steering of the primary model's capabilities and alignment properties.
proposals.method: We will pair a large language model (LLM) with a smaller, specialized critic model trained on human annotations to detect failures across dimensions like factuality, safety, and reasoning. Using techniques inspired by rejection sampling and distribution matching, we will develop an algorithm that inverts critic scores to generate data curation directives (e.g., up-weighting, synthesizing, or retrieving corrective examples). This engine will iteratively update a multi-source dataset, and we will measure the primary model's trajectory through periodic fine-tuning and evaluation on held-out critic and benchmark tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, automated framework for data-centric model steering, moving beyond static fine-tuning. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility of using learned critics for continuous dataset evolution and yield insights into the dynamics of model alignment through controlled distribution shifts.
proposals.novelty: While critics are commonly used for evaluation or reward modeling, their systematic inversion to drive dataset evolution for model steering is unexplored. This approach merges the paradigms of agent-based critique (as in RLAIF) and dynamic dataset curation into a single, closed-loop system for foundational model alignment.

proposals.title: Debiasing the Feedback Loop: A Causal Framework for Auditing Curator Influence in Self-Improving Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern instruction-tuned and agentic models increasingly rely on iterative feedback loops, where human or algorithmic curators filter outputs to create refined training data. This curation, while improving surface quality, injects latent curator preferences, creating a distributional shift from the model's original output space. Without accounting for this, models risk overfitting to curator bias, misrepresenting their true capabilities, and amplifying hidden preferences in deployed systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the curation process as a causal intervention, we can estimate and correct for its biasing effect, leading to models whose performance gains reflect genuine capability improvements rather than learned curator artifacts.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework treating curator selection as a treatment variable. Using propensity scoring on model output features (e.g., verbosity, style, confidence), we will estimate the probability of curation. This will allow us to re-weight curated data to approximate the original output distribution. We will then train models on this counterfactually adjusted dataset, comparing them to models trained on naively curated data. The approach will be validated using controlled synthetic curation policies and real human feedback datasets (e.g., HelpSteer), measuring performance on held-out, uncurated benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, causal methodology for auditing feedback loops in language model training, moving beyond qualitative concerns to quantitative measurement. It will deliver a practical toolkit for researchers to diagnose and mitigate curation bias, promoting more transparent and robust model self-improvement.
proposals.novelty: While propensity scoring is established in other fields, its application to model the *curator* as an explicit, biasing agent within AI training loops is novel. This shifts the perspective from curation as pure signal to curation as a measurable confounder requiring correction.

proposals.title: Auditing Data Curation Pipelines via Simulator Generalization Stress Tests
proposals.motivation: Modern LLM training increasingly relies on curated or filtered web-scraped data, often using LLM-based feedback to select high-quality examples. However, it is unclear if this curation produces robustly high-quality data or merely data optimized for the narrow distribution of the curation benchmark. This creates a risk of 'feedback bubbles' where training data quality is fragile, undermining model generalization and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: If an LLM-based feedback simulator, trained on data curated by a specific pipeline, fails to generalize its quality judgments to a broad, complex distribution of instruction-following tasks, then the underlying curation process has likely created a fragile benchmark-specific bubble rather than learning generalizable principles of quality.
proposals.method: We will construct a 'stress-test' distribution of multi-turn, multimodal, and adversarial instruction-following tasks, inspired by interactive agent benchmarks. We will train a feedback simulator (e.g., a reward model or critique generator) on data curated by a target pipeline (e.g., from Anthropic's HH or OpenAI's InstructGPT datasets). We will then measure the simulator's performance degradation and hallucination rates on the stress-test distribution versus its training distribution, using metrics from related work on optimal paraphrasing and prompt robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a novel diagnostic tool for evaluating data curation pipelines, moving beyond static benchmark scores. It contributes a methodology to quantify the fragility of learned feedback, offering actionable insights for building more robust training datasets and, by extension, more generalizable AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies simulator alignment or data filtering, this proposal uniquely repurposes simulator generalization failure as an audit mechanism for the upstream data curation process itself, creating a closed-loop evaluation for training data ecosystems.

proposals.title: CURATE: A Framework for Curated, Interactive Feedback in Agentic Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for training large language models (LLMs) relies heavily on static, passively scraped internet data and human-annotated datasets, which are costly and do not scale. This creates a disconnect between training data and the dynamic, multi-turn interactions that define real-world AI agent deployment. As models grow more capable as interactive agents, using their own raw, uncurated outputs for further training risks amplifying errors and hallucinations, degrading performance over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training system which interleaves model generation with an automated, interactive curation module—designed to filter, verify, and paraphrase outputs—will produce more robust, truthful, and instruction-following agents compared to training on uncurated self-generated data.
proposals.method: We will develop CURATE, a training loop where an LLM agent generates responses to a diverse instruction set. Each output is processed by a 'Curation Module' built on a smaller, verifier model trained for fact-checking and paraphrase generation (inspired by [PAUSE]-injected techniques). This module will classify outputs as 'high-quality', 'needs paraphrasing', or 'discard', creating a filtered training distribution. We will experiment with multi-stage training, starting with human feedback data and gradually blending in the curated synthetic data, measuring the impact on benchmarks for reasoning (GSM8K), hallucination (TruthfulQA), and interactive task success (WebShop).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a scalable framework for generating high-quality, interactive training data, reducing reliance on static scrapes; 2) empirical evidence on how curation quality affects the stability and capabilities of iteratively trained agents; and 3) a new dataset of machine-generated, machine-curated interaction traces for analysis.
proposals.novelty: While dataset filtering is known, CURATE integrates curation as an interactive, *online* component within the agent training loop itself, explicitly tackling the feedback source problem for agentic models. It combines instruction-following, hallucination mitigation, and multi-turn interaction into a unified data generation pipeline.

proposals.title: Meta-Gated Feedback: Preventing Negative Transfer in Open-Loop Cross-Task Influence
proposals.motivation: As AI systems scale, transferring knowledge across tasks via open-loop feedback (e.g., from a source model's outputs or gradients) is crucial for efficient learning. However, pre-2026 work on transfer and multi-task learning shows that naive cross-task influence often causes negative transfer, degrading target task performance. A principled method to filter feedback is needed to enable safe, automated knowledge sharing in agent and instruction-following systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned gating module, co-trained with the primary models, can accurately predict the impact of proposed cross-task feedback and selectively permit only those updates that lead to positive transfer, thereby safeguarding against performance degradation.
proposals.method: We will implement a lightweight neural gating network that takes as input task embeddings (from model activations), gradient alignment metrics, and performance heuristics. This gate will be meta-trained using a bilevel optimization loop: the inner loop applies gated feedback to update a target model on a validation batch, and the outer loop updates the gate's parameters to maximize the target's post-update performance. Training will use a multi-dataset benchmark (e.g., mixtures of instruction-tuning and agent interaction tasks) to learn generalizable gating criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for safe, open-loop knowledge transfer between AI systems, reducing the need for costly online interaction or human oversight. It will advance the field of robust multi-task and agent learning by introducing a trainable, adaptive mechanism to mitigate negative transfer.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed similarity measures or heuristics for transfer, our approach meta-learns the gating policy directly from performance outcomes, making it adaptive and context-aware. This integrates the 'hallucination' and robustness themes from the researcher's prior work into the core transfer mechanism.

proposals.title: Ecological Succession in Model Training: A Framework for Cross-Task Knowledge Infusion via Pioneer-Successor Conditioning
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task and transfer learning paradigms, such as fine-tuning or multi-task training, operate under closed-loop assumptions where a single model is optimized for related tasks. This fails to capture more open-ended, real-world learning where solving one problem fundamentally alters the data landscape for a subsequent, distinct problem. A formal framework for this open-loop, cross-task influence is needed to improve sample efficiency and enable more adaptive, sequential learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained on a source ('pioneer') task transforms raw data into a structured representation 'environment' that can facilitate more efficient learning of a different target ('successor') task, and that the degree of facilitation can be predicted by analyzing the pioneer's training dynamics and architectural inductive biases.
proposals.method: We will formalize the pioneer-successor framework using a two-stage pipeline. First, we train a transformer-based 'pioneer' model (e.g., a vision encoder or language model) on a source task. We then freeze its weights and use its intermediate layer representations as the sole input features for training a new 'successor' model from scratch on a distinct target task. We will systematically vary pioneer architectures (e.g., ViT, ConvNet), source tasks (e.g., image classification, syntactic parsing), and target tasks (e.g., visual reasoning, semantic role labeling) to measure successor performance. Analysis will focus on metrics of representation 'habitability' like dimensionality, task-relevant information, and noise profiles, correlating them with training efficiency gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel ecological analogy and formal framework for cross-task knowledge infusion, moving beyond fine-tuning. 2) Empirical benchmarks quantifying how different pioneer tasks and architectures prepare representation habitats for successors. 3) Practical guidelines for designing efficient sequential task curricula in open-ended learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in explicitly decoupling the pioneer and successor models into an open-loop, conditioning pipeline, contrasting with joint training or parameter-sharing methods. It introduces ecological succession as a principled analogy to study the directional, stage-wise preparation of representation spaces for entirely new tasks.

proposals.title: Open-Loop Feedback for Cross-Task Generalization: Using Hallucination Signatures to Improve Instruction Following
proposals.motivation: Current AI training paradigms, especially for instruction-following agents, operate largely in closed loops where feedback is task-specific. This limits the transfer of learned robustness across tasks. Pre-2026 work on hallucination detection and multi-task datasets provides a foundation, but a systematic method for leveraging signals from one task domain (e.g., factual QA) to improve performance on a structurally different task (e.g., procedural instruction) is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that statistical signatures of model failure (e.g., hallucination distributions) in one task domain can serve as effective open-loop feedback, guiding data curation or loss re-weighting to improve robustness and generalization in a different, target task.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-dataset benchmark with paired tasks (e.g., knowledge-intensive QA and procedural planning). Using methods from Taori's prior work on hallucination detection and [PAUSE]-injected paraphrasing, we will extract failure mode distributions from a source task model. These distributions will then be used to algorithmically re-weight or synthesize challenging examples for the target task's training data. We will train and evaluate instruction-tuned models (e.g., based on Llama or T5 architectures) on the target task, comparing standard training against our open-loop feedback-driven training protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and empirical evidence for cross-task open-loop feedback. It will yield a novel data curation algorithm and demonstrate improved generalization in instruction-following agents, moving beyond single-task optimization. The resulting benchmark will facilitate future research in systemic knowledge transfer.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common, using failure analytics from one independent task as a supervisory signal for a different task is novel. This explicitly relaxes the closed-loop assumption, creating a new feedback pathway between disparate model capabilities.

proposals.title: Attractor Basins for Factual Stability: Dynamical Systems Analysis of Multi-Model Feedback Loops
proposals.motivation: As AI-generated content proliferates online, it is increasingly mixed into future training datasets, creating a feedback loop between models and their data. Pre-2026, research on hallucinations and factual drift has focused on single-model behavior, but the systemic risks of multi-model ecosystems are poorly understood. This work addresses the critical need to ensure long-term factual fidelity in a world where models continuously learn from each other's outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multi-model ecosystems possess distinct dynamical attractors in their collective factual state space, and that specific prompting strategies and dataset mixing protocols can steer the system towards a high-fidelity attractor, preventing error cascades.
proposals.method: We will construct a simulated ecosystem of 3-5 instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., variants of LLaMA, Flan-T5). Using a curated multi-dataset of factual queries, we will run iterative generations where a model's output can be added to the training pool for the next 'generation' of other models. We will analyze the resulting trajectories using tools from dynamical systems (e.g., phase portraits, Lyapunov exponents) and graph theory to map the topology of factual stability. The [PAUSE]-injected paraphrasing method will be tested as an intervention to stabilize prompts.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first formal framework for analyzing factual stability as a collective, dynamical phenomenon in AI ecosystems. It will yield concrete, empirically validated protocols for dataset curation and prompt design that promote ecosystem-wide robustness, moving beyond single-model correction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the unit of analysis from the individual model to the interconnected ecosystem, applying dynamical systems theory to identify attractors for truth—a concept not explored in prior hallucination or multi-agent research.

proposals.title: Feedback Ecosystems in Multi-Agent AI: Dynamics, Degeneration, and Distributional Stability
proposals.motivation: The AI training pipeline is evolving from a single-model, human-supervised paradigm to a complex ecosystem where multiple AI agents generate content, critique each other, and produce data for future training cycles. Pre-2026, the risks of model collapse and hallucination amplification in single-model self-training loops are recognized, but the dynamics of multi-model, heterogeneous feedback ecosystems remain unexplored. This gap is critical as real-world AI deployment increasingly involves ensembles and interacting agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multi-model feedback ecosystems exhibit distinct stability regimes and that unguided interaction leads to predictable distributional shifts—specifically, a convergence towards 'lowest common denominator' outputs and an amplification of subtle, shared biases—which degrade performance on tail tasks and increase systemic hallucination.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled simulation environment where 3-5 instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., variants of Llama 2, T5, and GPT-style models via API) interact in defined roles (generator, refiner, critic). Using a multi-dataset approach (e.g., Alpaca, Flan, and a held-out human benchmark), we will simulate iterative training loops by mixing original human data with synthesized inter-agent outputs. We will analyze the dynamics using metrics for distribution shift (e.g., KL divergence, precision/recall against the original data manifold), hallucination rates (extending 'Sorry, Come Again?'-style prompting for detection), and emergent specialization or degeneration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for analyzing stability in multi-agent AI data ecosystems. It will yield concrete guidelines for ecosystem design (e.g., optimal mixture ratios, agent diversity requirements) to mitigate degenerative feedback. The findings will directly inform the development of safer, more robust training pipelines for interactive agent foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies single-model self-training or human-AI feedback, this proposal uniquely investigates the emergent, system-level properties arising from heterogeneous AI agents training on each other's outputs, a realistic but unmodeled scenario in pre-2026 AI development.

proposals.title: Auditor-Guided Synthetic Data Curation: A Closed-Loop Framework for Safe Multimodal Model Self-Improvement
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of AI training with synthetic data, especially for multimodal models, risks catastrophic feedback loops where errors and hallucinations are amplified. Pre-2026, methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) rely on costly human oversight and lack scalable, automated mechanisms to ensure the integrity of the data used for iterative training. A systematic framework to curate and validate AI-generated outputs before they enter training pipelines is a critical unsolved problem for sustainable advancement.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop training system, augmented by a parallel ecosystem of smaller, specialized auditor models that enforce dynamic quality thresholds, can enable safe and stable self-improvement for large multimodal models by preventing distributional collapse and contamination from low-quality synthetic data.
proposals.method: We will design a system where a primary multimodal model's outputs (text, code, images) are processed by a suite of smaller, task-specific auditor models, trained to detect hallucinations, factual inconsistencies, and distributional shifts. These auditors, inspired by adversarial filtering and quality estimation techniques, will assign confidence scores. A dynamic gating module, using algorithms like adaptive thresholding based on online quality metrics, will decide which outputs are admitted to a synthetic training dataset. This curated dataset will then be used for the next training cycle, creating a closed loop. Human reviewers will provide gold-standard labels to periodically retrain the auditors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, implementable architecture for safe synthetic data curation, 2) a benchmark and metrics for evaluating the long-term stability of self-improving AI systems, and 3) open-source tools for auditor model training and dynamic threshold adjustment, directly addressing concerns in scalable alignment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses post-hoc filtering or human-in-the-loop RLHF, our approach proactively integrates a mandatory, automated curation layer *before* training data is generated, and dynamically adjusts admission policies based on real-time distributional analysis, moving beyond static reward models.

proposals.title: Ecological Succession for Model Scaling: Progressive Synthetic Data Curation for Multimodal Agent Training
proposals.motivation: Scaling large multimodal models is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality, perfectly aligned image-text-code data. Current synthetic data generation, while promising, suffers from error propagation and hallucination, where poor outputs from one model iteration degrade the next. A principled, self-improving curation process is needed to break this cycle and enable reliable scaling of interactive agent capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: By structuring synthetic data generation as a progressive facilitation loop inspired by ecological succession—where initial 'pioneer' model outputs are programmatically curated to create a stable foundation for training more complex 'successor' models—we can create a self-improving data ecosystem that systematically reduces hallucination and enhances multimodal coherence.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-stage pipeline. First, a base model (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM) generates initial 'pioneer' synthetic text/code, which is rigorously filtered using rule-based and model-based verifiers (inspired by 'Sorry, Come Again?' prompting for hallucination reduction). This curated data trains a 'facilitator' model to generate more sophisticated, structured multimodal outputs (e.g., image-text pairs). These outputs are then filtered via a learned 'distribution' scorer, trained on a small seed of human-verified data, to select the highest-quality samples for the next training cycle, iteratively increasing data complexity and model capability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired framework for scalable synthetic data curation, directly addressing the hallucination and quality decay problems in iterative model training. It will deliver a practical methodology and open-source toolkit for generating high-fidelity multimodal training datasets, advancing the development of more reliable and capable interactive agent foundation models.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal analogy to ecological succession, translating the biological principle of progressive environmental facilitation into a concrete, algorithmic data curation and model training loop. This moves beyond simple iterative filtering to a structured, stage-gated process where each model iteration has a defined 'ecological' role.

proposals.title: AuditNet: A Self-Improving Multimodal Feedback Loop for Robustness Evaluation of Vision Models
proposals.motivation: Despite high accuracy on static benchmarks, vision models fail unpredictably in real-world deployment due to distribution shifts and edge cases not captured in datasets like ImageNet. Current robustness evaluation is manual, slow, and lacks semantic diversity. This creates a critical gap between lab performance and real-world reliability, hindering safe deployment in autonomous systems and assistive technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: A large multimodal model (LMM), acting as an automated robustness auditor and iteratively refined on its own discovered failures, can generate semantically richer and more challenging test distributions than static datasets, thereby exposing and helping to mitigate previously unknown failure modes in target vision models.
proposals.method: We will instantiate a closed-loop system with a frozen target vision model (e.g., a CLIP-based classifier) and a trainable LMM auditor (e.g., based on Flamingo or BLIP-2 architecture). The LMM will generate diverse, coherent multimodal probes: text descriptions of edge-case scenarios and corresponding synthetic images (via a diffusion model like Stable Diffusion guided by the LMM's text). The target model's performance on these probes is evaluated. Failures are used to create corrective feedback pairs (e.g., 'probe that caused failure' -> 'corrected label/explanation'), which are incorporated into the LMM's training via instruction tuning, improving its auditing precision over cycles.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for automated, semantically-aware robustness auditing that moves beyond pixel-level perturbations. 2) A self-improving dataset of challenging multimodal probes that evolves to find novel failures. 3) Empirical evidence on the efficacy of closed-loop feedback for improving evaluation distribution design, with implications for more robust model training pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While LMMs are used for generation and evaluation separately, this work uniquely integrates them into a *closed-loop, self-improving audit system* where the auditor's training distribution is dynamically shaped by the failures it discovers, a concept distinct from static red-teaming or fixed adversarial attack libraries.

proposals.title: M3F: Multimodal Model Feedback for Self-Improving Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current large multimodal models (LMMs) are trained on static, human-curated datasets, limiting their ability to self-correct and improve post-deployment. As LMMs generate increasingly complex outputs (text, code, images), creating scalable, high-quality feedback for iterative training is a major bottleneck. This research addresses the critical need for automated, in-domain feedback loops to enable continuous model enhancement without proportional growth in human annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal foundation model can be used to generate high-quality, multi-format (e.g., scores, corrections, refinements) feedback on its own generations, and training on a filtered distribution of this feedback data will lead to measurable improvements in output quality and a reduction in hallucinations across modalities.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the self-training and self-critique principles from unimodal LLMs to a multimodal setting. Using a frozen, capable LMM (e.g., GPT-4V or a large open-source variant) as a 'feedback teacher,' we will generate a large, diverse dataset of (generation, feedback) pairs across text, code, and image domains. The feedback will be structured using prompt templates inspired by 'Sorry, Come Again?' techniques, injecting verification steps. A smaller, trainable 'student' LMM will then be fine-tuned on a curated subset of this data, selected via quality heuristics and agreement metrics, creating a scalable feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework and empirical evidence for scalable multimodal self-improvement. It will yield a publicly released dataset of multimodal feedback and demonstrate a training recipe that reduces hallucination rates and improves output coherence across modalities in the student model, advancing towards more autonomous and capable agent AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While self-training exists for language, applying it systematically to generate and utilize *cross-modal* feedback (e.g., an image critique generated from a text instruction) at the foundation model scale is unexplored. The core novelty is the closed-loop, multi-format feedback generation and curation pipeline specifically designed for multimodal model outputs.

proposals.title: The Data Feedback Loop: Quantifying the Effect of Model-Generated Data on Architectural and Methodological Diversity
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of AI increasingly relies on synthetic or model-generated data for training, creating a potential feedback loop. Prior to 2026, research has focused on data quality and scaling laws, but the systemic impact of this data homogenization on the diversity of model architectures and training methodologies remains unexplored. This is critical for understanding long-term innovation risks, as reduced diversity could lead to brittle, monolithic progress in AI development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iterative training on model-generated data creates a homogenizing pressure, leading to a measurable convergence in architectural choices (e.g., transformer variants) and training techniques (e.g., instruction-tuning paradigms) across the field, thereby reducing the explored design space.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled simulation of the research ecosystem. Starting with a diverse seed of architectures (Transformers, State Space Models, Hybrids) and training methods (supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, direct preference optimization), we will iteratively generate synthetic data using the best-performing model from each generation. This data will then be used to train the next generation of models. We will track diversity metrics across generations, including architectural hyperparameters, loss landscape characteristics, and output distribution entropy on held-out human-curated benchmarks. The analysis will leverage concepts from Rohan Taori's prior work on multi-dataset training and hallucination measurement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for quantifying the 'diversity collapse' in AI research driven by data feedback loops. It will yield concrete metrics to assess the health of the research ecosystem and inform strategies for maintaining architectural and methodological innovation. The findings will contribute directly to the literature on robust and sustainable AI development.
proposals.novelty: While existing work studies how models affect data, we study the inverse: how this data recursively shapes the models and methods the community builds. This shifts the focus from single-model performance to the evolutionary dynamics of the entire research paradigm.

proposals.title: Active Meta-Labeler: A Sequential Bayesian Framework for Stabilizing Human-in-the-Loop Feedback Systems
proposals.motivation: The effectiveness of instruction-tuned and RLHF-trained models critically depends on the quality and stability of human feedback. However, human labelers exhibit temporal drift, varying biases, and inconsistencies, which can corrupt the training signal and lead to unreliable or harmful model behavior. Existing systems treat human feedback as a static gold standard, lacking mechanisms to dynamically assess and stabilize the labelers themselves.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling individual human labelers as non-stationary, noisy sensors via sequential Bayesian inference and actively managing task allocation based on this model will produce a more robust and consistent training signal, leading to automated feedback policies with higher reliability and lower hallucination.
proposals.method: We will develop an active meta-learning framework. Each human labeler is modeled with a Bayesian Neural Network (BNN) that estimates their reliability and bias over time, updated via sequential Monte Carlo methods. An anomaly detection module (Isolation Forest) flags sudden drifts. A task allocator, formulated as a multi-armed bandit problem, dynamically routes queries to counteract instabilities and maximize consensus. The stabilized labels train an ensemble of automated feedback policies, whose predictive uncertainty is used to recursively query the human system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, operationalizable framework for modeling human labeler dynamics in real-time, 2) a dual-stabilization architecture where human and automated systems co-evolve, and 3) empirical evidence that stabilizing the human component reduces downstream model hallucination and improves robustness in interactive agents.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that filters or aggregates static human labels, our approach actively models and intervenes on the human labeler process itself in a closed loop. It integrates sequential Bayesian state estimation with active learning specifically for the meta-problem of labeler reliability.

proposals.title: Gain Scheduling for AI Training: A Human-in-the-Loop Controller to Stabilize Unsupervised Feedback Loops
proposals.motivation: Modern AI training increasingly relies on automated feedback loops, such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or AI-generated data pipelines. These loops can become unstable, leading to reward hacking, distributional collapse, or catastrophic forgetting, which degrades model performance and safety. Current solutions often require continuous, expensive human oversight or lack a principled framework for intervention, limiting scalability and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the AI training process as a dynamical system and implementing a gain-scheduling-inspired human-labeling controller, we can strategically inject human-labeled data at detected instability regimes to correct and stabilize the feedback loop, enabling more robust training with minimized human oversight.
proposals.method: We will define instability metrics (e.g., reward variance, gradient norm explosion, or confidence entropy) to partition the training state-space into operational regimes. A scheduler, inspired by control-theoretic gain scheduling, will monitor these metrics in real-time. Upon crossing predefined instability thresholds, it will trigger the sampling of human-labeled data from a curated, multi-distribution dataset (e.g., a mix of high-quality instruction data and adversarial examples). We will test this on an instruction-tuned language model fine-tuned via online RLHF, comparing the scheduler's stabilization efficacy against baseline continuous or random human feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for integrating human oversight into automated AI training, grounded in control theory. It will yield a concrete method to improve training stability and sample efficiency in feedback-driven systems, contributing to both the AI alignment and interactive agent research communities.
proposals.novelty: While human-in-the-loop AI is well-studied, the explicit adaptation of control-theoretic gain scheduling to dynamically schedule human intervention based on real-time stability metrics is a novel cross-disciplinary analogy. This moves beyond static data mixing or fixed intervention schedules.

proposals.title: Stabilizing Self-Improving LLMs via Strategic Human-in-the-Loop Feedback Curation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) trained via iterative self-improvement (e.g., using reinforcement learning from AI feedback, RLAIF) are prone to instability, reward hacking, and distributional collapse, where the model's own generated data degrades its performance. This is a critical bottleneck for developing robust, aligned AI agents. Pre-2026, methods to stabilize these systems without massive, undirected human labeling remain under-explored.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic, targeted human labeling of feedback data—focusing on edge cases and points of high model uncertainty—can correct reward model drift and stabilize the training dynamics of automated feedback loops more effectively than uniform or fully automated approaches.
proposals.method: We will simulate an unstable RLAIF loop using a base LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2) and a trainable reward model. The core experiment will compare three data sampling strategies for human labeler intervention: 1) random baseline, 2) uncertainty sampling based on reward model entropy, and 3) a novel distribution-aware sampling that identifies response clusters where the AI feedback distribution diverges from a held-out human reference. Human labels will be injected into the training set at each iteration, and we will track stability metrics (policy KL divergence, reward model accuracy on a validation set, and downstream task performance) against a fully automated control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for efficient human-in-the-loop stabilization of self-improving AI systems. It will yield concrete metrics for detecting feedback loop instability and a publicly released dataset of strategic human feedback interventions. The findings will inform the design of more reliable and sample-efficient agent training pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While human-in-the-loop RL is known, the novelty lies in framing human intervention as a *stabilization mechanism for automated feedback systems* and in developing a sampling strategy that targets the *dual problem* of reward model drift and policy collapse simultaneously, moving beyond simple uncertainty sampling.

proposals.title: ALIGN: Adaptive Language-agent Instruction with Generative Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for interactive agents, as surveyed in recent work, often fail to robustly align their multi-step actions with nuanced human intent, leading to inefficiency and hallucinated behaviors. Existing instruction-tuning methods primarily use static, pre-collected datasets, which lack the dynamic, corrective feedback crucial for teaching agents to recover from errors in real-world tasks. This limits their practical deployment in assistive or collaborative settings where human expectations are complex and situational.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating iterative, generative human feedback—where humans provide natural language corrections and refinements—into the training loop of an interactive agent foundation model will significantly improve its alignment with human intent and reduce action-level hallucination compared to static dataset training.
proposals.method: We will extend an instruction-tuned language model (e.g., based on LLaMA or T5 architectures) serving as an agent's policy. The method involves a two-phase loop: 1) The agent interacts within a simulated environment (e.g., ALFRED, WebShop) and proposes action sequences; 2) A human trainer provides free-form textual feedback on these actions via an interface, which is used to generate synthetic corrective examples. We will employ a "PAUSE"-inspired paraphrasing model to diversify this feedback and create a multi-turn, distributionally robust training dataset. This dataset will then be used for further Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) or reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to fine-tune the agent policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable framework for integrating real-time human feedback into agent training, moving beyond static demonstrations. It will provide empirical evidence on how generative feedback affects alignment and hallucination metrics in interactive tasks. The resulting public dataset of human-agent feedback interactions will be a valuable resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While RLHF is used for language alignment, its application for training multi-step, embodied agent policies with free-form natural language corrections is nascent. Our approach uniquely combines optimal paraphrasing for feedback augmentation with preference optimization, specifically targeting the reduction of action hallucination in sequential decision-making.

proposals.title: Active Query Optimization for Human Feedback in Agent Training
proposals.motivation: Training interactive AI agents with human feedback is crucial for alignment and capability, but it is prohibitively expensive and data-inefficient. Current methods, like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), often rely on static or randomly sampled feedback datasets, which may not target the most informative or challenging scenarios for the agent. This work addresses the critical bottleneck of efficiently acquiring high-value human feedback to train more robust and capable agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing human feedback collection as an active learning problem, we can strategically query human trainers for feedback on agent trajectories that maximize information gain or uncertainty reduction, leading to more sample-efficient agent training and improved final performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt pool-based and stream-based active learning strategies (e.g., uncertainty sampling, query-by-committee, expected model change) to the sequential decision-making space of an agent foundation model. The core technical approach involves training a proxy reward model on an initial seed of human preferences, then using this model's uncertainty estimates over a large, unlabeled distribution of agent interaction trajectories (e.g., from a simulator or logged data) to select the most ambiguous episodes for human feedback. This iterative loop of agent interaction, uncertainty-based query selection, human labeling, and reward model retraining will be rigorously evaluated against passive sampling baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and practical algorithms for active human feedback collection in agent training. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency gains of such methods and release a benchmark for active preference optimization in interactive agent domains, advancing the field beyond static dataset curation.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-established in supervised learning, its systematic application to optimize expensive human-in-the-loop feedback for training foundation model-based agents is novel. This bridges a gap between classical machine learning strategies and modern, large-scale agent training pipelines.

proposals.title: Curriculum for Cognition: Efficient Language Model Training via Dynamic Data Selection and Pruning
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for achieving broad AI capability is scaling data and compute, which is environmentally unsustainable and economically prohibitive. This research addresses the critical need for more efficient training paradigms by drawing inspiration from how humans learn complex skills through structured curricula and focused practice, rather than exhaustive exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: Intelligently sequencing training data via a curriculum based on complexity and learning progress, combined with active pruning of redundant or uninformative examples, can achieve comparable model capability and robustness to massive-scale training while significantly reducing computational cost.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage framework. First, we will develop a data complexity metric, potentially based on model perplexity, syntactic features, or learned embeddings, to automatically rank examples. Second, we will implement a dynamic curriculum scheduler that adjusts the data distribution based on model performance, introducing 'desirable difficulties' akin to hard negative mining. We will integrate this with a pruning algorithm that identifies and removes redundant data points from the training stream, using techniques from core-set selection or gradient-based data valuation, to create a continuously refined, high-quality training corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable methodology for data-efficient model training, directly challenging the scale-only narrative. It will yield open-source tools for data curation and curriculum scheduling, and empirical evidence on the performance-efficiency frontier for modern language models.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and data selection are known, this proposal uniquely combines them into a dynamic, closed-loop system for large-scale pre-training, explicitly framed through the lens of educational science and 'desirable difficulty' to strategically induce robust learning, moving beyond simple filtering by quality scores.

proposals.title: The Living Benchmark: A Human-AI Adversarial Ecosystem for Continuous Evaluation of Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Static benchmarks for foundation models are quickly saturated, failing to capture emergent failure modes in deployment. This creates a dangerous gap between reported performance and real-world robustness, especially for interactive agents. A continuous, adaptive evaluation framework is needed to proactively identify risks as models evolve and are integrated into complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop, multi-modal adversarial ecosystem, combining automated red-teaming with human-in-the-loop challenge generation and real-world failure monitoring, will surface a more diverse and severe set of model vulnerabilities than static benchmarks, providing a truer measure of real-world robustness.
proposals.method: We will build a hybrid platform where: 1) An automated adversarial model, trained via reinforcement learning on a distribution of known failures, generates novel textual and multi-modal prompts. 2) Human red teamers interact with this system through a structured interface, guided to exploit speculative failure modes, with their successful attacks added to the training dataset. 3) We instrument deployed agent models to log anomalous interactions and feed these back as seeds for the adversarial generator, creating a continuous challenge loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source platform for continuous model evaluation, a new dataset of dynamic adversarial challenges, and quantitative evidence of the gap between static and adaptive testing. It will provide a methodology for ongoing safety and capability assessment throughout a model's lifecycle.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior red-teaming efforts, this creates a perpetual, closed-loop ecosystem that directly integrates real-world deployment signals and human creativity to drive benchmark evolution, moving beyond one-off challenge sets.

proposals.title: Systematic Threat Modeling for Vision-Language Models: Discovering Adversarial Attack Surfaces via Instruction-Image Failure Maximization
proposals.motivation: As multimodal foundation models become increasingly integrated into interactive agent systems, their security and robustness are critical. Pre-2026 adversarial research has focused on unimodal perturbations or simple cross-modal conflicts, lacking a systematic framework to discover the complex, semantic-level vulnerabilities that arise from the intersection of language instructions and visual content. A proactive, threat-modeling approach is needed to map these failure modes before deployment in high-stakes applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a fixed instruction, an algorithmic search over the image space can reliably generate or retrieve images that maximize a model's failure probability, thereby revealing systematic, semantically meaningful attack surfaces that are not discoverable via random sampling or gradient-based image attacks alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will use a large-scale, multi-source dataset of instruction-image pairs to establish a baseline failure distribution. Second, we will implement an iterative optimization loop using a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) guided by a failure maximization objective. This objective will be defined by the negative log-likelihood of a correct response from a target VLFM (e.g., LLaVA, Flamingo), encouraging the generation of images that induce hallucinations or comprehension failures for the fixed instruction. We will analyze the resulting failure-maximizing pairs to categorize attack surface types.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for automated adversarial attack surface discovery in VLFMs, 2) a structured catalog of high-failure instruction-image pairs that constitute a threat model, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal robustness based on semantically hard, systemically discovered failures, moving beyond simple perturbation tests.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying cybersecurity threat modeling principles to multimodal AI security, moving from crafting individual adversarial examples to systematically discovering entire failure regions in the joint embedding space through guided generation, rather than direct pixel manipulation.

proposals.title: Self-Distilling Hallucination-Aware Specialists: Automated Error Curation for Reliable Domain Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to generating plausible but incorrect 'hallucinations,' which severely limits their trustworthiness in specialized domains like medicine or law. Creating robust, domain-specialized models typically requires vast, manually annotated datasets to identify and correct these errors, creating a significant scalability bottleneck. This work addresses the need for automated, scalable methods to build efficient, reliable models without exhaustive human supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: A large source model can be used to automatically generate a rich, self-supervised training signal for hallucination robustness by analyzing its own generation variations, confidence patterns, and internal contradictions. Distilling a smaller model with this signal will produce a specialist that is more factually reliable and better calibrated within its target domain than a standard knowledge-distilled counterpart.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline where a large 'teacher' model (e.g., LLaMA-2, GPT-4) generates multiple responses to domain-specific queries. We will then apply consistency checks (e.g., semantic entropy), prompt-based verification (inspired by [PAUSE]-injected paraphrasing), and analysis of internal token probabilities to automatically label hallucination severity and type. This curated dataset will train a 'student' model via a modified distillation loss that heavily penalizes identified hallucination patterns, incorporating confidence calibration objectives. The final model will be a compact, domain-adapted transformer optimized for factual fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for creating trustworthy, domain-specialized models without manual hallucination annotation. It will contribute a new method for self-supervised error categorization and a distillation technique that explicitly optimizes for reliability over mere performance mimicry.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard knowledge distillation or dataset filtering, this approach uses the teacher model's internal and behavioral signals to *automatically* construct a 'hallucination-aware' curriculum. It moves beyond compressing capability to actively reshaping the student's generation profile towards higher factual precision.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization for Inverse Data Reconstruction: A Query-Efficient Attack on RLHF-Tuned Model Privacy
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a critical but opaque component in aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values. While prior work has demonstrated membership inference and data extraction attacks on pre-trained models, the privacy implications of the RLHF fine-tuning stage remain poorly understood. Assessing whether RLHF inadvertently memorizes and exposes sensitive human feedback or proprietary data is crucial for responsible deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Bayesian optimization (BO) can be effectively formulated as an active inference engine to solve the inverse problem of reconstructing influential training data points from RLHF-tuned models, doing so with significantly greater query efficiency than gradient-based or random search attacks.
proposals.method: We will treat the RLHF-tuned model as a black-box function mapping input prompts to a scalar objective (e.g., likelihood of a target output, or activation magnitude in a specific layer). A Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate will model this function, and an acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement) will guide the sequential search for prompts that maximize the objective, indicating proximity to memorized data. We will benchmark against established extraction methods (e.g., adversarial attacks, beam search) on models like LLaMA-2-Chat, using datasets with known canaries to measure reconstruction fidelity and query cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for auditing the data privacy of aligned LLMs, specifically isolating risks introduced by RLHF. It will yield a benchmark for query-efficient data reconstruction attacks and could inform the development of more privacy-preserving alignment techniques.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying Bayesian optimization—traditionally used for hyperparameter tuning—to the inverse problem of data extraction in RLHF models, creating a synergy between optimization theory and privacy attacks that is more query-efficient than existing methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Data Attribution: Reconstructing Training Data Distributions from Foundation Model Behavior
proposals.motivation: Foundation models are trained on massive, often undisclosed datasets, raising critical questions about data provenance, copyright, and the origins of model biases or capabilities. Current auditing methods are limited to membership inference or detecting specific memorized examples. A systematic method to deduce the *compositional distribution* of training data from a finalized model would enhance transparency and accountability in AI development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the behavioral patterns of a foundation model—specifically its response distributions across diverse, strategically designed prompts—contain sufficient statistical signal to reconstruct key attributes of its training data mixture, including the relative prevalence of different data sources and domains.
proposals.method: We will treat this as an inverse optimization problem. First, we will generate a large, multi-domain probe dataset covering text, code, and multimodal instructions. We will then measure the model's behavioral 'fingerprint' via its output distributions (e.g., log-probabilities, embedding shifts) on these probes. Using techniques from optimal transport and distribution matching, we will iteratively adjust the weights of candidate source datasets (e.g., The Pile, Common Crawl snapshots) to minimize the divergence between the probe-response distribution of the model-under-test and a proxy model trained on the weighted mixture. The core algorithm will adapt Sinkhorn iterations for this high-dimensional, discrete setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for model auditing, moving beyond single-example memorization to characterize training data mixtures. It will establish a methodology for data provenance analysis in black-box settings and contribute to the scientific understanding of how data distributions imprint on model behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on extracting individual training examples or on membership inference, our approach is novel in aiming to reconstruct the aggregate statistical makeup of the training corpus through a principled inverse problem framework, linking macroscopic behavior to data composition.

proposals.title: Adversarial Scenario Discovery: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for High-Stakes Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: As language models are deployed in high-stakes domains, standard benchmarks fail to expose critical failure modes like subtle overconfidence and context-sensitive hallucinations. Current red-teaming methods are either purely automated, generating low-quality adversarial examples, or rely on unstructured human effort, which does not scale. A systematic, scalable method to discover and formalize a model's most severe weaknesses is urgently needed for reliable evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework that uses automated probes to identify a model's performance boundaries and then guides a structured human panel to author scenarios targeting those boundaries will produce a higher-signal, more challenging, and continuously evolving evaluation suite than purely automated or unstructured human methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase system. First, an automated module uses techniques like distributional adversarial attacks and multi-turn dialogue stress tests to map model confidence and error patterns across diverse datasets. Second, these 'weakness signals' are presented via a structured interface to a curated human panel (domain experts, ethicists, writers) who author high-quality, edge-case scenarios. The process iterates, with new model versions evaluated on the accumulated suite, and new weaknesses from those evaluations guiding further human authoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, scalable framework for high-stakes evaluation that combines automated discovery with structured human expertise, 2) a public, evolving benchmark suite of high-quality adversarial scenarios, and 3) new insights into the relationship between model overconfidence, hallucination, and failure modes in complex, instruction-following tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior red-teaming, this approach uses automated analysis not to generate final test cases, but to strategically guide and scale high-value human creativity, creating a feedback loop where evaluation sophistication grows with model capability.

proposals.title: CRITIC-ADV: A Closed-Loop System for Automated Model Hardening via Adversarial Critic Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models exhibit complex, semantically grounded failure modes like hallucination and reasoning errors that are not addressed by traditional adversarial attacks based on norm-bounded perturbations. Current methods for improving robustness rely on static, human-curated datasets, which cannot dynamically adapt to a model's evolving weaknesses. This creates a critical need for automated systems that can continuously discover and patch these high-level vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model trained as a 'critic' to audit and describe failure modes in a target model can generate sophisticated, black-box adversarial examples that, when used for adversarial training, will create a self-improving loop that significantly enhances the target model's robustness against semantically meaningful errors.
proposals.method: We will train a critic model (a decoder-only LLM) on a multi-task dataset of (model input, erroneous output, critique) tuples to learn to identify and articulate failures in a target ASR or VLM. The critic will then guide a black-box adversarial example generator using methods like AutoPrompt or PEZ, optimizing inputs to induce the failures it predicts. These adversarial examples will form a dynamic dataset for adversarial training (e.g., using TRADES or MART) of the target model. The critic will be periodically updated on the target model's new errors, closing the loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for automated, iterative model hardening that moves beyond low-level perturbations. It will produce new benchmarks for semantic adversarial robustness and demonstrate a scalable method for reducing hallucinations and systematic errors in foundation models without exhaustive human oversight.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of a trainable, generative critic model with adversarial training in a closed loop, shifting adversarial example generation from low-level signal corruption to the targeted exploitation of learned, high-level semantic weaknesses.

proposals.title: Measuring and Mitigating Cross-Modal Hallucination in Multimodal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: As multimodal foundation models (e.g., GPT-4V, LLaVA, Flamingo) become central to interactive agent systems, their tendency to generate content inconsistent with provided visual or auditory context—cross-modal hallucination—poses a critical reliability risk. This problem is under-explored compared to text-only hallucination, yet is more dangerous for real-world applications like assistive robotics or multimodal instruction-following, where fabricated perceptions can lead to unsafe actions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cross-modal hallucination stems from a training distribution mismatch where models over-rely on strong text priors, and that targeted interventions during instruction tuning can systematically reduce these errors without degrading overall model capability.
proposals.method: We will first construct a multi-task benchmark, building on the 'Sorry, Come Again?' framework, to quantify hallucination across image-to-text, text-to-image, and audio-to-text tasks. Using a model like LLaVA or OpenFlamingo as a base, we will analyze error distributions to identify failure modes. We will then develop and train a 'consistency critic' module that scores alignment between generated content and input modalities. This module will be integrated via a reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)-inspired pipeline, using the critic's scores as a reward to fine-tune the model towards cross-modal faithfulness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel benchmark and metrics for evaluating cross-modal hallucination, 2) An open-source 'consistency critic' training framework adaptable to various multimodal architectures, and 3) Empirical evidence on the efficacy of distribution-aware fine-tuning for improving multimodal reliability, directly advancing the development of trustworthy interactive agents.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies hallucination in text or single modalities, our approach systematically defines, measures, and mitigates *cross-modal* hallucination, explicitly tackling the interplay between modalities that is central to next-generation agent AI.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Real-Time Audio Adversarial Attacks
proposals.motivation: Adversarial attacks on Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems expose critical security vulnerabilities, but most research focuses on offline, static audio. Real-world threats often involve streaming audio with dynamic conditions, a setting where current perturbation methods underperform. Developing agents that can adaptively attack in real-time is crucial for understanding and ultimately defending modern, interactive AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent, trained via a structured curriculum on progressively complex simulated streaming environments, will learn to generate more effective and transferable real-time adversarial audio perturbations than methods trained on static datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a PPO-based RL agent that interacts with a simulated streaming ASR environment (e.g., using a pre-2026 model like Wav2Vec 2.0). The curriculum will start with simple tasks (e.g., perturbing fixed phrases with white noise) and advance to complex scenarios involving variable latency, background noise, and multiple speakers. The agent's actions will be parameterized perturbations applied to a short audio buffer, with reward based on ASR transcription error. We will leverage multi-task and distributional training techniques to enhance robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for generating real-time, adaptive adversarial audio, demonstrating higher success rates against streaming systems. It will provide new insights into the vulnerabilities of causal, temporal models under interactive attack. The resulting simulated training environment and curriculum blueprint will serve as a benchmark for future research in adversarial robustness for sequential data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of curriculum learning and interactive RL specifically for the causal, real-time adversarial audio domain, moving beyond static optimization to train an adaptive agent that discovers temporal attack strategies.

proposals.title: ALIGNER: Adversarial Policy Learning for Iterative Cross-Modal Domain Alignment
proposals.motivation: Multimodal foundation models rely on aligned embedding spaces, but the modality gap remains a key challenge for robust generalization. Current contrastive and adapter-based alignment methods are largely static and one-directional, lacking a mechanism to actively probe and adapt to the most critical points of semantic misalignment. An adaptive, iterative process that discovers these failure modes is needed to build more invariant and semantically coherent joint representations.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing cross-modal alignment as a reinforcement learning problem, where an agent learns to generate targeted adversarial perturbations, will discover non-obvious, semantically meaningful transformations that more effectively bridge the modality gap than static alignment losses.
proposals.method: We will train an RL agent (policy network) using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to generate adversarial perturbations for image or text inputs. The state is the current joint embedding from a frozen pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP), the action is a perturbation vector, and the reward is a composite signal from alignment metrics (e.g., cosine similarity gain for matched pairs, loss for mismatched). This creates a closed-loop system where the policy's 'attacks' drive the iterative refinement of a lightweight alignment projector network via an adversarial loss, inspired by GAN dynamics but with RL-guided exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel adaptive framework for cross-modal alignment that simultaneously learns an attack policy revealing critical misalignments and uses it to train more robust representations. It will provide new insights into the structure of the modality gap and deliver a method for improving the semantic invariance of multimodal embeddings for downstream tasks like retrieval and agent interaction.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is recasting modality alignment as an RL-driven adversarial learning problem, moving beyond static loss functions to an interactive, probing process. This integrates adversarial robustness and representation learning in a novel loop, distinct from prior GAN-based or simple adversarial training methods for domain adaptation.

proposals.title: MIMIC: Multi-Image Masked Instruction Corruption for Testing and Fortifying Vision-Language Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in multi-image, instruction-following contexts, such as interactive agents and visual storytelling. However, their robustness against adversarial attacks that exploit sequential, cross-image reasoning remains underexplored. This work addresses a critical gap in safety and reliability by proposing attacks that are perceptually subtle yet semantically potent, mimicking real-world deceptive manipulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distributing minimal, psychoacoustically-inspired visual perturbations across a sequence of images can create a novel class of adversarial instructions that reliably corrupt a model's cross-image reasoning, and that defending against such attacks will require and inspire new training paradigms that enhance relational robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, for attack generation, we adapt saliency-based visual masking models (inspired by JPEG and audio codecs) to craft perturbations that are imperceptible when an image is viewed in isolation but are optimized to disrupt features critical for multi-image fusion in models like Flamingo or GPT-4V. We will use projected gradient descent (PGD) to optimize these perturbations across image sets, guided by a loss that maximizes divergence from the correct instruction-following output. Second, for defense, we will create a novel multi-image adversarial training dataset and fine-tune models on these 'masked adversarial instructions' to improve resilience.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel benchmark for multi-image adversarial robustness, 2) A new attack strategy based on perceptual masking, expanding the adversarial toolkit beyond single-image Lp-norm bounds, and 3) Preliminary defense strategies that could lead to more reliable multi-modal agent systems, directly advancing the safety themes in Rohan Taori's prior work on hallucination and interaction.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of psychoacoustic/visual masking principles with multi-instance adversarial attacks, creating context-dependent perturbations that exploit the temporal and relational structure of sequential visual input—a dimension largely absent from prior single-image adversarial literature.

proposals.title: Robustness-Aware Black-Box Fine-Tuning via Natural Evolution Strategies
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised pre-training imbues models with useful invariances that confer robustness to natural distribution shifts. However, standard gradient-based fine-tuning for downstream tasks often catastrophically degrades this robustness, leading to brittle task-specific models. There is a critical need for adaptation methods that preserve a model's inherent robustness while optimizing for task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) for black-box fine-tuning can directly optimize for a composite objective of task accuracy and robustness, thereby preserving the beneficial invariances learned during self-supervised pre-training better than gradient-based methods.
proposals.method: We will treat a frozen, pre-trained model (e.g., a Vision Transformer or LLM) as a black-box function. Using NES, we will iteratively propose small parameter perturbations, evaluate each perturbed model on a multi-distribution evaluation suite (e.g., ImageNet variants for vision, or diverse instruction-following datasets for language), and compute a fitness score combining primary task accuracy and performance on shifted data. The parameter distribution will be updated via the NES gradient estimate to maximize this robustness-aware fitness, requiring only forward passes and performance feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a practical, black-box paradigm for robustness-aware model adaptation, applicable to any pre-trained model. It will provide empirical evidence that direct optimization of non-differentiable robustness metrics during fine-tuning can mitigate robustness collapse, a key failure mode of current methods.
proposals.novelty: While NES has been used for RL and hyperparameter tuning, its application to explicitly preserve pre-training invariances during fine-tuning via a composite robustness objective is novel. This approach circumvents the need for differentiable robustness losses or access to model gradients.

proposals.title: Dual-Phase Adversarial Training for Robust Automatic Speech Recognition
proposals.motivation: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems are increasingly deployed in safety-critical and interactive agent applications, yet remain vulnerable to adversarial audio perturbations that are imperceptible to humans. This fragility undermines trust in voice-controlled systems and poses security risks. Robust ASR is therefore essential for the reliable deployment of human-AI interaction systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-phase adversarial training regimen, which first uses black-box attacks to generate a diverse distribution of adversarial examples and then fine-tunes a model on this augmented dataset, will yield ASR models with significantly improved robustness against a wide range of audio perturbations without sacrificing performance on clean speech.
proposals.method: We will first generate a large, multi-style adversarial dataset by applying black-box adversarial attacks (e.g., based on genetic algorithms or boundary attacks) to a pre-trained ASR model (e.g., Whisper) using a clean speech corpus like LibriSpeech. In the second phase, we will adversarially train a model by interleaving batches from this adversarial dataset with clean data, employing a training objective that combines standard cross-entropy loss with a robustness-promoting term (e.g., TRADES). The model architecture will be a conformer-based encoder-decoder, standard for state-of-the-art ASR.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a systematic framework for generating black-box adversarial audio examples at scale, 2) a robust ASR model benchmarked against diverse audio attacks, and 3) empirical analysis of the trade-offs between clean accuracy and adversarial robustness in speech systems, directly informing the development of more reliable interactive agents.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is known in vision, its application to ASR has been limited and often uses simple, white-box attacks. Our approach is novel in its explicit focus on leveraging a *distribution* of black-box adversarial examples—which are more realistic threats—to train a robust model, aligning with Rohan Taori's recent methodological focus on multi-dataset training and system robustness.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Language-Supervised Learning via Multi-Agent Synthetic Data Curation and Hallucination-Aware Training
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale language-supervised models require massive, noisy web-scraped datasets, which are expensive to collect and can embed harmful biases. This paradigm limits accessibility and control. A method to achieve high performance with smaller, curated datasets would democratize model development and enable safer, more specialized AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent synthetic data generation framework, guided by instruction-following and optimized to minimize hallucination, can produce a high-quality, compact training dataset that matches or exceeds the performance of models trained on orders-of-magnitude larger web-scale corpora.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where a population of lightweight agent AI models (based on an interactive agent foundation model) engages in a collaborative, instruction-driven dialogue to generate and refine synthetic (text, image) pairs. A central 'critic' agent, trained with [PAUSE]-injected optimal paraphrasing techniques, will score outputs for factual consistency and instruction alignment, creating a hallucination-aware training signal. This iterative process will create a high-quality, multi-modal dataset, which will then be used to train a model with a distribution-aware contrastive loss that emphasizes hard negatives from the synthetic data.
