proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for generating high-fidelity, small-scale datasets for vision-language training, 2) empirical evidence on the scaling laws of model performance versus curated dataset size, and 3) new techniques for integrating hallucination mitigation directly into the data generation pipeline.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and synthetic data are known, our approach uniquely combines a multi-agent generative process with a hallucination-aware filtering mechanism specifically designed for instruction-following data, moving beyond simple paraphrasing or back-translation.

proposals.title: CurriculumCLIP: Curriculum Learning for Vision-Language Alignment by Progressive Difficulty
proposals.motivation: Training robust vision-language models like CLIP requires large, noisy web datasets where caption complexity and image-text alignment difficulty vary widely. Standard training treats all examples equally, which may lead to inefficient learning and increased hallucination, where models generate plausible but ungrounded captions. A curriculum that strategically orders training data could improve sample efficiency and final model fidelity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, which sequences training examples from easy to hard based on caption complexity and alignment difficulty, will lead to vision-language models that learn more efficiently, achieve higher downstream performance, and exhibit reduced hallucination compared to models trained on randomly ordered data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage difficulty scoring system for (image, text) pairs. First, a linguistic complexity score will be derived from pre-trained language models (e.g., GPT-3) assessing caption syntactic and semantic density. Second, an alignment difficulty score will be computed using a small, frozen 'teacher' CLIP model's contrastive loss on the pair. We will train a CLIP-style model from scratch on LAION-5B, progressively exposing it to batches ordered by a weighted sum of these scores, starting with simple, well-aligned examples and gradually introducing more complex and challenging pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for applying curriculum learning to large-scale vision-language pre-training. We expect to demonstrate improved convergence speed and final zero-shot accuracy on benchmarks like ImageNet and COCO. A key analysis will measure the reduction in hallucination on compositional reasoning tasks, contributing to more reliable multimodal agents.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is established in NLP and vision, its application to web-scale multimodal pre-training is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in our automated, dual-faceted difficulty metric for web data and the systematic investigation of its impact on foundational model efficiency and hallucination, a core concern in Rohan Taori's recent work on agent interaction and prompt engineering.

proposals.title: Efficient-CLIP: Training Small-Scale Vision-Language Models with Synthetic Instruction Data and Distribution Matching
proposals.motivation: CLIP models are foundational for multimodal AI, but their large size and reliance on massive, curated web-scale image-text datasets limit deployment in resource-constrained or data-scarce environments (e.g., on-device, specialized domains). Pre-2026, efficient vision-language models are underexplored, and scaling down CLIP without catastrophic performance loss remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small CLIP variant can achieve robust alignment by training on a strategically generated, multi-source synthetic dataset of image-instruction pairs and by explicitly optimizing for distributional consistency between teacher and student model outputs.
proposals.method: We will design a compact dual-encoder architecture using efficient backbones (e.g., MobileNetV3, TinyViT). To overcome caption scarcity, we will generate a diverse training corpus by: 1) using large language models (LLMs) to synthesize descriptive captions and visual instructions for existing images, and 2) applying optimal paraphrasing techniques (inspired by 'Sorry, Come Again?' Prompting) to augment text data. Training will employ a multi-task objective combining contrastive loss with a distribution-matching loss (e.g., KL divergence) against a frozen, large CLIP teacher to preserve semantic knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a family of highly efficient, deployable CLIP models and a methodology for training them with limited real captions. It will provide new insights into data-efficient vision-language alignment and the transfer of multimodal knowledge to compact architectures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of LLM-synthetic instruction data and explicit output distribution matching for small CLIP training, moving beyond simple distillation and curated web data, which is non-obvious given the prevailing scale-up paradigm in pre-2026 multimodal research.

proposals.title: Simulating the Human in the Loop: A Generative Model for Adversarial and Low-Quality Feedback
proposals.motivation: The quality and reliability of human feedback is a critical bottleneck in training and aligning AI systems via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and related methods. Pre-2026, research focuses on simulating idealized, helpful human preferences, but real-world data collection is plagued by adversarial, inconsistent, or low-effort annotations that degrade model performance. Understanding and mitigating the impact of these undesirable feedback patterns is essential for building robust, real-world AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model, trained on multi-dataset distributions of real human feedback, can accurately simulate specific undesirable feedback patterns (e.g., adversarial, biased, or low-quality). Further, we hypothesize that training language models on a mixture of simulated desirable and undesirable feedback will improve their robustness and reduce susceptibility to manipulation.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional language model (e.g., based on a T5 or GPT-2 architecture) to act as a 'simulated human' that generates feedback. The model will be conditioned on a target response and a 'human behavior tag' (e.g., 'adversarial', 'inattentive', 'biased'). Training data will be constructed by programmatically labeling examples from existing public human feedback datasets (e.g., Anthropic's HH-RLHF, OpenAI's Summarize) and instruction-tuning datasets based on annotation heuristics and metadata. We will then integrate this simulator into a training loop, using it to generate synthetic, undesirable feedback for a target LLM during instruction tuning or RLHF, employing techniques like distributionally robust optimization to harden the model against such inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, publicly available generative model for simulating non-ideal human feedback, 2) a systematic framework for analyzing how different feedback pathologies affect model training, and 3) new training recipes that improve model robustness by explicitly accounting for adversarial or low-quality human input during alignment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses simulators to model *idealized* human preferences for data augmentation, our proposal inverts this target to explicitly model and study *undesirable* human behaviors. This shifts the focus from pure data scaling to understanding and improving training dynamics under realistic, noisy human-in-the-loop conditions.

proposals.title: Beyond Pairwise: Scaling Language Model Alignment with Simulated Multi-Modal Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current methods for aligning LLMs with human preferences, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), rely heavily on costly pairwise comparison data. This format is informationally sparse and fails to capture the richer, more nuanced feedback humans naturally provide, such as rankings, scores, and textual corrections. Incorporating these modalities could lead to more sample-efficient and effective alignment, a critical bottleneck in developing safe and capable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Simulating and integrating richer human feedback modalities—specifically ranked lists, numerical ratings, and free-form textual corrections—within a synthetic interaction framework like AlpacaFarm will yield language models that align more accurately and efficiently with human preferences than models trained solely on pairwise comparisons.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to generate not just pairwise preferences but also k-wise rankings, scalar reward scores (via a learned reward model), and corrective text edits. For training, we will adapt and benchmark algorithms like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for ranking data, develop a multi-task objective combining reward modeling and sequence-level policy optimization for scores, and integrate a supervised fine-tuning stage for textual corrections. The core technical challenge is designing a unified training loop that robustly learns from this heterogeneous, simulated feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable methodology for leveraging richer human feedback, moving beyond the pairwise paradigm. We will release an open-source extension to AlpacaFarm with new simulation protocols and training code, enabling the community to explore more efficient alignment. The resulting models will be evaluated on standard benchmarks for helpfulness, harmlessness, and instruction-following to demonstrate concrete gains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored individual feedback types in isolation, this proposal systematically integrates multiple modalities within a single, scalable synthetic training framework. It directly addresses the information-efficiency gap in RLHF by simulating a more realistic distribution of human feedback, a non-trivial extension of existing preference simulation techniques.

proposals.title: Generalization of LLM-as-a-Judge: Evaluating Simulated Feedback on a Broad Distribution of Complex Instruction-Following Tasks
proposals.motivation: The use of large language models (LLMs) as simulators to provide automated feedback (e.g., for training or evaluation) is a promising but brittle paradigm. Current methods are typically validated on narrow, often academic, task distributions, raising concerns about their reliability for real-world, diverse applications. This research directly addresses the critical gap in understanding whether LLM-simulated feedback generalizes to more complex and varied instruction-following scenarios, which is essential for building robust AI systems and training pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance and alignment of LLM-simulated feedback will degrade significantly when applied to a broader, more complex distribution of instruction-following tasks compared to its initial training or validation set, due to distributional shift and increased task ambiguity.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-dimensional task distribution spanning complexity axes such as multi-step reasoning, multimodal grounding (text+code), and open-ended creativity, building on datasets like MMLU, Big-Bench Hard, and instruction variants of coding benchmarks. We will implement a state-of-the-art LLM-as-a-Judge simulator (e.g., using GPT-4 or Llama 2/3 with chain-of-thought prompting) to generate feedback scores. The core validation will involve correlating simulator scores with a ground-truth metric—human expert ratings—across this distribution, analyzing performance drop-offs via task attribute regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of the generalization limits of LLM-based feedback simulators, yielding a diagnostic framework for task distributions where such simulators fail. It will produce concrete guidelines for researchers and practitioners on when and how to reliably deploy simulated feedback in training loops or evaluation systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for feedback on curated sets, our study explicitly tests the boundaries of simulator reliability under controlled distributional shift, linking failure modes to specific task complexities—a crucial step for advancing robust, scalable AI training and evaluation methodologies.

proposals.title: Multi-Aspect, Distribution-Aware Evaluation (MADE): A Framework for Robust Automatic Assessment of Language Model Outputs
proposals.motivation: Current automatic evaluation metrics for language models, such as BLEU or ROUGE, often correlate poorly with nuanced human judgments, especially for open-ended generation tasks. This discrepancy hinders reliable model development and benchmarking, as improvements in metrics may not reflect genuine qualitative gains. The problem is exacerbated by the rise of instruction-tuned models and AI agents, whose outputs must be evaluated across diverse criteria like correctness, coherence, and instruction-following.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-faceted evaluation framework, which decomposes quality into distinct, learnable aspects and explicitly models the distribution of possible valid responses, will yield automatic scores with significantly higher correlation to human judgments than existing one-dimensional metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop MADE, a training-based evaluation system. First, we will create a multi-aspect human-annotated dataset, extending concepts from instruction-tuning datasets to label outputs for correctness, coherence, conciseness, and instruction adherence. Second, we will train a suite of lightweight, specialized evaluator models (e.g., based on T5 or DeBERTa) on these aspects, using contrastive learning to distinguish between high- and low-quality examples. Third, inspired by 'distribution' and calibration techniques in pre-2026 work, we will incorporate a reference-free plausibility module that scores how likely a response is given the input, conditioning on a diverse set of model-generated candidates to capture response variety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a publicly available, multi-aspect evaluation dataset; 2) the MADE framework, a set of open-source, plug-and-play evaluators; and 3) empirical evidence showing improved human-correlation across diverse text generation tasks, including those involving AI agents and long-form dialogue. This will enable more reliable and efficient model development cycles.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored learned metrics or single-aspect evaluation, MADE's novelty lies in its integrated, distribution-aware approach that explicitly decomposes and models multiple quality dimensions simultaneously, directly addressing the multifaceted nature of human judgment that simple string-overlap or likelihood metrics miss.

proposals.title: Policy-Augmented Feedback Simulation: Closing the Loop for Robust Language Agent Training
proposals.motivation: Training interactive agents via methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) relies heavily on high-quality, diverse preference data. Current approaches often use static feedback simulators or human annotations, which are costly, limited in scale, and can introduce distributional mismatch with the agent's evolving policy. This creates a bottleneck for scalable and robust agent alignment, particularly in complex, open-ended instruction-following tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent's own trained policy model contains latent knowledge about task structure and human preferences that can be leveraged to iteratively improve the feedback simulation process, leading to a more effective and data-efficient training loop.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-loop framework. First, we train an initial policy model (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM) on a seed dataset of (instruction, response, feedback) tuples. Second, we deploy this policy in a critique generation mode: given an instruction and a candidate response from a baseline feedback simulator, the policy generates targeted critiques or suggests more informative alternative queries. These policy-generated meta-feedbacks are used to refine the simulator's outputs (e.g., via prompt-tuning or as training data for a separate critique model) or to directly augment the preference dataset for the next round of policy training, creating a self-improving cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for bootstrapping high-quality preference data, reducing reliance on extensive human annotation. It will demonstrate measurable improvements in agent performance and reduction in harmful hallucination on benchmarks like MT-Bench and HELM, providing a pathway toward more autonomous and scalable alignment pipelines for interactive AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses policies to generate responses or uses simulators to train policies, the key novelty is the bidirectional use of a policy to *critique and improve the simulator itself*, framing feedback generation as an optimizable component within the training distribution.

proposals.title: InstructGen: Inverting the Evaluation Paradigm for Instruction-Following Models via Multimodal Instruction Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of instruction-following models is largely forward-facing, assessing how well a model's output matches a given instruction. This fails to diagnose the model's internal representation of the instruction itself. An inverse task—generating the instruction from an image and a model's response—can probe model comprehension and expose systematic hallucination patterns, providing a novel diagnostic for alignment and robustness in agent foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model to reconstruct the instruction from a (multimodal input, model output) pair will yield a powerful diagnostic tool that reveals the latent instruction representations within foundation models, and that the quality of reconstruction will correlate with the original model's tendency to hallucinate or deviate from intended task constraints.
proposals.method: We will curate a multi-task, multimodal dataset of (image, instruction, model response) triples using models like the Interactive Agent Foundation Model. A separate transformer-based encoder-decoder model, 'InstructGen', will be trained to take the image (via a frozen CLIP encoder) and the agent's response as input, and generate the original instruction. We will experiment with contrastive and optimal paraphrasing techniques (inspired by [PAUSE] prompting) to handle instruction distribution diversity and improve reconstruction fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel inverse evaluation paradigm for instruction-following systems. It will provide a diagnostic framework to quantify instruction fidelity and pinpoint sources of hallucination. The resulting 'InstructGen' model could also serve as a data augmentation tool for generating diverse, grounded instructions.
proposals.novelty: While instruction-following is well-studied, systematically inverting the process to diagnose model understanding from its outputs is unexplored. This approach uniquely bridges training evaluation, hallucination analysis, and instruction dataset curation into a single, interpretable framework.

proposals.title: Robust Instruction Following Under Visual Degradation: A Multi-Dataset Study of Hallucination and Comprehension
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal instruction-following models are primarily evaluated on high-quality, curated images, creating an unrealistic performance benchmark for real-world applications where inputs are often low-resolution, occluded, or adversarially manipulated. This gap limits our understanding of model robustness and failure modes in practical settings. Addressing this is crucial for developing reliable agent AI systems that can operate outside of controlled lab environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art vision-language models will exhibit a significant and predictable increase in hallucination and a decrease in task comprehension when presented with degraded visual inputs, and that targeted training on a multi-distribution dataset of imperfect images can substantially mitigate these failures.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel multi-dataset benchmark comprising images systematically degraded via blurring, noise, occlusion, and adversarial patches (e.g., using Projected Gradient Descent). We will evaluate foundation models like GPT-4V and Flamingo on this benchmark using the 'Sorry, Come Again?' prompting framework to measure comprehension shifts and hallucination rates. Based on the failure analysis, we will fine-tune an interactive agent foundation model on a mixture of clear and degraded data, employing techniques like data augmentation and distribution-aware training to improve robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of how visual degradation impacts instruction-following and hallucination in multimodal agents. It will yield a public benchmark for robustness and a novel training methodology that enhances model reliability for imperfect real-world perception, directly advancing the development of more dependable interactive AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial robustness and image corruption benchmarks exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on their intersection with instruction-following and hallucination—a core challenge for agent AI—and proposes a mitigation strategy grounded in multi-dataset training and optimal paraphrasing prompts, moving beyond simple input testing.

proposals.title: AlignEval: Training a Multi-Dimensional Reward Model for Human-Aligned Instruction Following Assessment
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of instruction-tuned and agentic AI models has outpaced the evolution of their evaluation. Current automatic metrics like BLEU or ROUGE correlate poorly with nuanced human judgments on instruction-following quality, which includes aspects like correctness, completeness, and safety. This misalignment hinders rapid, reliable model development and benchmarking, creating a critical bottleneck in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A reward model trained on a multi-dimensional, fine-grained human feedback dataset can serve as a superior automatic evaluator, achieving significantly higher correlation with holistic human judgment on instruction-following tasks than existing reference-based or LLM-as-a-judge metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale, multi-dimensional human feedback dataset (AlignEval-Data) where annotators rate model outputs on specific axes like instruction adherence, hallucination, and helpfulness. Using this data, we will train a specialized transformer-based reward model (AlignEval-RM). Its architecture will incorporate techniques from related work on optimal paraphrasing and comprehension (e.g., PAUSE tokens) to better process and compare instruction-output pairs. The model will be trained via a contrastive loss, learning to rank outputs according to the aggregated human preference signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a public, multi-dimensional human feedback dataset for instruction-following evaluation, 2) a high-performance, open-source automatic evaluator (AlignEval-RM) that closely mirrors human preferences, and 3) an analysis of which quality dimensions are most critical for alignment. This will accelerate the training and iterative improvement of instruction-following models and AI agents.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic LLM-as-a-judge setups or single-score reward models, our approach explicitly models the multi-faceted nature of human judgment through structured data collection and a dedicated model architecture, moving beyond a monolithic 'quality' score. It directly leverages recent insights on reducing metric hallucination and improving comprehension for the specific task of evaluation.

proposals.title: Direct Preference Optimization for Faithful Instruction Following in Agentic Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often fail to follow complex, multi-step instructions faithfully, leading to hallucinations and unreliable behavior, especially in agent-based systems designed for real-world interaction. While Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has been used to align models with human preferences for safety and helpfulness, its direct application to optimize for precise, step-by-step instruction adherence remains underexplored. This gap is critical for developing robust interactive agents that can reliably execute tasks as specified by a user.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directly optimizing a language model's policy for human preference judgments on instruction-following fidelity, using a reward model trained on such preferences, will significantly reduce hallucination and improve the reliability of agentic behavior compared to standard supervised fine-tuning or general-purpose RLHF.
proposals.method: We will first create a high-quality, multi-turn instruction-following dataset where human annotators score agent responses on strict adherence to each instruction step. Using this dataset, we will train a specialized reward model to predict human preference scores for instruction fidelity. We will then apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) or a related offline RL algorithm to fine-tune a base model (e.g., derived from an Interactive Agent Foundation Model) directly against this reward model, circumventing the instability of online RL. The evaluation will benchmark against models fine-tuned with standard SFT and general RLHF on held-out instruction sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology and benchmark for using preference learning to directly optimize instruction-following accuracy. It will yield a model with demonstrably lower hallucination rates in agentic scenarios and release a novel human-annotated dataset for instruction-fidelity preference modeling. The findings will advance the goal of creating more predictable and trustworthy AI agents.
proposals.novelty: While RLHF is established for general alignment, this proposal specifically targets the precise, granular objective of instruction-following fidelity, integrating it with the emerging paradigm of agent foundation models. The novelty lies in the targeted reward modeling and the application of modern preference optimization algorithms to this specific, critical aspect of agent reliability.

proposals.title: Transferable Difficulty: A Curriculum Learning Framework for Scaling Instruction-Tuning Across Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is central to aligning LLMs with human intent, yet current methods often treat diverse benchmark tasks as a monolithic dataset, ignoring inherent difficulty gradients. This can lead to inefficient training and suboptimal generalization, particularly for complex, multi-step reasoning. A principled, transferable curriculum for instruction difficulty could streamline training pipelines and improve model robustness on challenging, out-of-distribution instructions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a difficulty curriculum learned from one set of instruction-tuning benchmarks can be transferred to efficiently train models on new, unseen benchmarks, leading to faster convergence and better final performance compared to standard random or fixed-curriculum training.
proposals.method: We will first develop a multi-faceted difficulty metric for instruction-response pairs, combining factors like required reasoning steps, specificity, and cross-entropy loss from a base model. Using this metric, we will apply curriculum learning (e.g., via a data scheduler) to train a model on a source benchmark suite (e.g., FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions). The core innovation is to then extract and formalize the learned curriculum—as a scoring function or ordered task taxonomy—and apply it to sequence a distinct target benchmark. We will test transfer efficacy on models like Llama-2 or T5, measuring convergence speed and final accuracy on held-out tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a generalizable framework for quantifying and transferring instructional difficulty, 2) empirical evidence on the transferability of learning curricula across task distributions, and 3) a practical method for more sample-efficient instruction tuning of foundation models, reducing computational cost.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been explored in narrow domains, applying it to organize broad-coverage instruction benchmarks and demonstrating cross-benchmark curriculum transfer is novel. This moves beyond single-dataset ordering to a meta-learning perspective on instructional difficulty.

proposals.title: Instruction-Family-Specialized Models: Scaling Data and Reducing Hallucination via Targeted Training
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are trained on broad, mixed instruction datasets, which can lead to suboptimal performance and increased hallucination on specific, high-stakes tasks like coding, reasoning, or safety-critical dialogues. As of 2025, the prevailing paradigm of a single, generalist 'instruction-tuned' model fails to exploit the potential depth of expertise achievable through specialization. This work addresses the need for more reliable and capable agents by investigating whether extreme specialization can push the boundaries of quality and robustness within defined task families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a separate, scaled-up model on a massively expanded dataset curated for a single instruction family (e.g., 'coding', 'creative writing', 'logical deduction') will yield superior performance and significantly lower hallucination rates within that family compared to a generalist model of equivalent total compute, and that these specialized models can be effectively integrated into a multi-agent system.
proposals.method: We will adopt the 70 instruction families from prior work (e.g., Alpaca, Super-NaturalInstructions) as our taxonomy. For each family, we will programmatically generate and collect orders-of-magnitude more synthetic examples, while rigorously filtering for quality using methods like [PAUSE]-injected paraphrasing for consistency checking. We will train a suite of 70 specialized decoder-only models (e.g., based on Llama 3 or Mistral architectures) from a common pre-trained checkpoint, using standard causal language modeling. The training will leverage multi-dataset mixtures but within the single-family domain. We will benchmark against generalist models like GPT-4 and Claude, and analyze hallucination using established metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical study on the limits of instruction-family specialization, producing a public suite of 70 high-performance specialized models. It will deliver a rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between specialization and generalization, and a framework for building compound AI systems that dynamically route queries to expert models, directly advancing the field of Agent AI.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task training is standard, the systematic creation of a *massive ensemble of deeply specialized models*, each trained on scaled-up, family-specific data, is a novel and underexplored alternative to the monolithic model paradigm. This approach uniquely combines scale (in data per family) with narrow focus, moving beyond simple prompting or adapter-based methods.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Pre-training as a Foundation for Robust Instruction-Following Agents
proposals.motivation: Large language and agent models often degrade in performance under natural distribution shifts, such as novel user instructions or unseen environmental contexts, leading to unreliable and hallucinatory behavior. This fragility limits the real-world deployment of interactive AI systems. While self-supervised learning (SSL) has proven effective for learning general representations in vision and language, its role in conferring robustness to instruction-following agents against multi-modal distribution shifts is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agent foundation models pre-trained with multi-modal self-supervision on diverse, unlabeled interaction data will learn more transferable and disentangled representations, leading to greater robustness to natural distribution shifts in instruction-following tasks compared to models trained primarily with supervised instruction-tuning.
proposals.method: We will extend an interactive agent foundation model architecture (e.g., based on Taori's prior work) and pre-train it using a multi-modal self-supervised objective combining masked modeling for language and visual tokens, and temporal contrastive learning for action sequences, on a large-scale dataset of human-environment interactions (e.g., Ego4D). We will then conduct controlled fine-tuning and evaluation on a suite of instruction-following benchmarks (e.g., ALFRED, BEHAVIOR) and systematically measure performance degradation under curated distribution shifts (novel object combinations, paraphrased instructions, unseen backgrounds). We will compare against a baseline model trained with equivalent compute but using only supervised demonstration data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for or against the robustness benefits of SSL for agent AI, moving beyond pure performance metrics. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating agent robustness to distribution shifts and could inform more efficient, reliable training pipelines for future interactive systems.
proposals.novelty: While SSL robustness is studied in static vision/language tasks, applying this lens to the sequential decision-making and instruction-following paradigm of agent AI is novel. The investigation specifically ties SSL pre-training objectives to the mitigation of hallucination and brittleness in interactive agents, a core challenge in the field.

proposals.title: Scaling Natural Robustness: Training Interactive Agent Models on Multi-Domain, Multi-Modal Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current large language and agent models, while powerful, exhibit brittleness and hallucination when faced with out-of-distribution or multi-modal real-world tasks. This limits their reliability and safety in open-ended, interactive systems. Training on larger but homogeneous datasets has plateaued in improving this natural robustness, creating a need for a qualitative shift in data diversity and scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a unified interactive agent foundation model on a curated, massive-scale dataset spanning diverse domains (e.g., code, science, dialogue, embodied tasks) and modalities (text, image, action sequences) will significantly enhance its natural robustness, reducing hallucination and improving generalization on novel, composite instructions.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-trillion token dataset, 'OmniMix', by programmatically integrating and filtering existing high-quality sources (e.g., code repositories, instructional videos with transcribed actions, textbooks, web dialogues). We will train a decoder-only transformer, architecturally similar to PaLM or GPT-4 but optimized for action prediction, on this dataset using next-token prediction. A key innovation is the integration of a dynamic data scheduler that adjusts the mixture of domains during training based on per-domain loss plateaus, and the injection of [PAUSE]-like paraphrasing tasks during fine-tuning to explicitly target hallucination reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new state-of-the-art benchmark for agent robustness across a suite of held-out, cross-domain tasks; 2) the open-sourced 'OmniMix' dataset curation pipeline and a large-scale model checkpoint; 3) an analysis of how data distribution scaling, beyond mere token count, affects emergent capabilities and failure modes in interactive agents.
proposals.novelty: While scaling model size is common, our focus is on systematic scaling and dynamic scheduling of *data domain diversity* for robustness, combined with targeted anti-hallucination techniques from the researcher's prior work, applied specifically to the emerging paradigm of interactive agent foundation models.

ideas.title: Apply cybersecurity threat modeling to foundation model security
ideas.description: Use adversarial threat modeling to systematically identify and mitigate novel attack surfaces.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

ideas.title: Apply algorithmic fairness interventions pre-emptively
ideas.description: Integrate bias mitigation techniques into the model to shape its outputs before they enter the data pool.

ideas.title: Test intervention on real-world web-scale data pipelines
ideas.description: Apply the proposed calibration intervention to large-scale, live data collection systems used by industry.

ideas.title: Co-Evolutionary Adversarial Attacks for Persistent Efficacy Against Adaptive Defenses
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for generating adversarial examples that remain effective against dynamically updated and adaptively hardened machine learning models. It employs a co-evolutionary training regimen where a population of attack strategies competes against a diverse and progressively fortified ensemble of surrogate defenses. The objective is to produce attacks that are not just efficient against a fixed target, but that generalize across model architectures, training data shifts, and common defensive adaptations like adversarial training or input filtering. This results in more robust and persistent attack methods that maintain a high success rate even as the target system evolves post-deployment.

ideas.title: Apply ecological resilience theory to AI system robustness
ideas.description: Use concepts from ecology to design AI systems resilient to distribution shifts and failures.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

ideas.title: Combine SCA with retrieval-augmented generation
ideas.description: Integrate SCA prompting with RAG to tackle hallucinations from both comprehension and knowledge gaps.
ideas.sourceTitle: "Sorry, Come Again?" Prompting - Enhancing Comprehension and Diminishing Hallucination with [PAUSE]-injected Optimal Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Transfer domain adaptation discrepancy measures
ideas.description: Use measures like MMD or Wasserstein distance to quantify shift and predict correlation strength.
ideas.sourceTitle: Accuracy on the Line: on the Strong Correlation Between Out-of-Distribution and In-Distribution Generalization

ideas.title: Develop theory for emergent capabilities in foundation models
ideas.description: Create theoretical frameworks to predict and explain emergent capabilities arising from scale.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

ideas.title: Robustness-Aware Black-Box Fine-Tuning via Natural Evolution Strategies
ideas.description: This research proposes using Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) to perform black-box fine-tuning of models that have been pre-trained with self-supervision. The core objective is to adapt these models to specific downstream tasks while explicitly optimizing for robustness to natural distribution shifts, a property inherent to the pre-training but often degraded by standard fine-tuning. Because NES requires only task performance feedback, it can optimize complex, non-differentiable robustness metrics (e.g., performance across multiple shifted datasets) directly, circumventing the need for gradient-based updates that may compromise learned invariances. This enables a novel paradigm of 'robustness-aware' black-box adaptation, where the model is steered based solely on its holistic behavioral robustness, preserving the benefits of self-supervised pre-training in deployment environments.

ideas.title: Develop theory for weak correlation cases
ideas.description: Create theoretical models explaining when ID and OOD performance correlation breaks down.
ideas.sourceTitle: Accuracy on the Line: on the Strong Correlation Between Out-of-Distribution and In-Distribution Generalization

ideas.title: Feedback Robustness in Simulated Niches: Testing if Synthetic Community Shifts Predict Real Model-Data Co-evolution
ideas.description: This research proposes using small, niche online communities as empirical testbeds to investigate whether robustness to synthetically generated distribution shifts (e.g., adversarial prompts, style perturbations) reliably predicts robustness to the organic, feedback-driven distribution shifts that occur when a model is deployed. By deploying language models in controlled community simulations or historical archives, we can measure performance decay under real feedback loops. We then correlate this with performance under a battery of synthetic shifts designed to mimic aspects of community evolution, thereby directly testing the proxy assumption in a concrete, measurable socio-technical system where the real shift mechanism (model-influenced user behavior) is known.

ideas.title: Adaptive Attack Optimization via Evolutionary Selection Pressure
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's directed evolution techniques, this future work aims to increase targeted attack success rates and reduce required generations. In directed evolution, applying a stringent, high-throughput selective pressure (e.g., linking enzyme function to survival) rapidly converges an evolving population to an optimal solution. Adapted to adversarial attacks, this involves designing an efficient, batch-based fitness function that evaluates attack success and applies strong selection: only the top-performing perturbations 'reproduce' to form the next generation, perhaps through recombination and focused mutation. This creates an evolutionary algorithm under intense selective pressure, specifically optimized to find successful adversarial examples with minimal generations, thereby improving both success rate and query efficiency.

ideas.title: Develop automated hallucination quantification and ranking method
ideas.description: Create a systematic method to quantify hallucination severity and rank LLMs based on the proposed spectrum.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Troubling Emergence of Hallucination in Large Language Models - An Extensive Definition, Quantification, and Prescriptive Remediations

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Stability Analysis to Data Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology to the original control theory concept for data feedback loops. In ecology, stability analysis and resilience theory are used to understand and manage complex feedback systems like predator-prey dynamics, identifying tipping points and designing interventions to maintain ecosystem stability. The analogous solution involves modeling these interactions and applying concepts like resilience basins and indicators of critical transitions. Adapted to the research context, this means modeling the data feedback loop in ML/AI systems (where a model influences the data it will later be trained on) as an ecological system. The model and data distribution are treated as interacting species. The adapted idea is to use ecological stability tools—such as analyzing Lyapunov exponents for the coupled dynamics, mapping resilience landscapes of different model states, and monitoring for early-warning signals like critical slowing down—to predict instability or collapse (e.g., model polarization, runaway feedback). This allows for the design of 'controller' interventions inspired by ecological management, such as introducing curated 'keystone' data or managed 'disturbances' (e.g., data refresh protocols) to maintain a desired, stable performance regime.

ideas.title: Compositional Skill Scaffolds for Robust, Specialized Model Efficiency
ideas.description: This research develops methods to create efficient, domain-specialized systems that avoid the brittleness of pure distillation. Instead of compressing knowledge into a single small network, the approach constructs a generative library of explicit, modular skill programs (e.g., code snippets, symbolic solvers) for a target domain. A compact neural model acts as a controller, learning to identify novel problems and dynamically retrieve and compose these skills. The skill library is continuously curated by querying a large foundation model with domain-specific edge cases, ensuring the system remains robust to new challenges while operating with the efficiency of a small model.

ideas.title: Pre-emptive Model Immunization via Bias Antigens
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of pre-emptive immunization from vaccinology to algorithmic fairness. In vaccinology, a weakened pathogen antigen is introduced to train the immune system for future encounters. The analogous solution is to create 'bias antigens'—curated datasets exemplifying known societal biases. The adapted research idea involves integrating a controlled training phase where the model is exposed to these bias antigens, stimulating it to build robust internal mechanisms to recognize and reject such patterns. This 'immunizes' the model, pre-emptively shaping its outputs to be fairer before they are generated and enter the external data pool, preventing the propagation of biased data.

ideas.title: Physically Consistent and Adaptively Robust Adversarial Examples
ideas.description: This research creates adversarial attacks that remain effective after passing through unknown, complex real-world capture and processing chains. It moves beyond digital noise models by incorporating a differentiable simulator of physical sensor artifacts and common human-editing operations into the attack generation loop. The approach uses test-time estimation to adapt the perturbation to the specific distortions observed in a feedback loop, ensuring robustness to both environmental variability and deliberate attempts to break the attack. This results in adversarial examples that are consistently potent from digital creation through physical realization and subsequent manual or automated processing.

ideas.title: Low-Dimensional Manifold NES with Surrogate Amortization for Efficient Black-Box Optimization
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the sample inefficiency of high-dimensional NES by performing gradient estimation within a dynamically constructed, low-dimensional subspace, such as a random projection or the span of recent gradient directions. A fast surrogate model, like a shallow neural network or Gaussian process, is continuously updated with the evaluated points to predict and refine the gradient, amortizing the cost over many steps. This hybrid system maintains the black-box, gradient-free advantage of NES while achieving sample efficiency comparable to approximate gradient-based methods, making it viable for optimizing large neural networks or complex simulation parameters where true gradients are inaccessible.

ideas.title: Incorporate psychoacoustic masking for imperceptibility
ideas.description: Apply psychoacoustic models from audio coding to better hide adversarial perturbations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Targeted Adversarial Examples for Black Box Audio Systems

ideas.title: On-Device, Self-Diagnosing Autoregressive Models for Reliable Edge Inference
ideas.description: This research develops lightweight, self-diagnosing autoregressive (AR) models specifically for deployment on edge devices. The core innovation is the co-design of model compression techniques with embedded diagnostic modules that continuously monitor optimization health and output calibration during inference. This allows the model to detect when its predictions become unreliable due to data drift, quantization errors, or other on-device constraints. The result is a system that provides not just predictions, but also a real-time confidence measure, enabling safer and more robust autonomous operation in resource-constrained environments.

ideas.title: Mitigate hallucinations via grounded environments
ideas.description: Explore how embodied, grounded interaction reduces factual errors from large foundation models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning and Selective Data Pruning for Efficient Model Training
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that scale (in data and compute) is the only path to broad AI capability by drawing an analogy from developmental psychology and educational science. The analogous problem—how humans achieve broad competence without experiencing all possible data—is solved through structured curriculum learning and focused practice on 'desirable difficulties.' We adapt this solution by proposing a research agenda to design and test progressive training curricula for large models, starting with high-quality, foundational corpora and strategically introducing complexity. The core hypothesis is that intelligent data selection and ordering, coupled with algorithms to prune redundant data, can achieve similar breadth and robustness as massive-scale training but with drastically improved efficiency. This explores alternative training paradigms that prioritize data quality and learning sequence over sheer scale.

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Curation via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea investigates feedback loops for scaling large multimodal models by drawing an analogy from ecological succession. In restoration ecology, pioneer species prepare the environment for more complex communities. Analogously, we propose a solution where a model's initial generations (text/code) are programmatically curated to create structured, high-quality synthetic training data—acting as 'pioneer species.' This curated data then trains the next model iteration to generate more sophisticated and coherent multimodal outputs (e.g., perfectly paired image-text samples), which in turn are filtered and used for further training. This adapted approach creates a controlled, progressive facilitation loop, scaling model capability by ensuring each training cycle builds upon a more stable and complex 'ecosystem' of synthetic data.

ideas.title: Adversarial Critic Models for Self-Improving Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research fuses model critique with adversarial training by developing a 'critic' foundation model trained to audit and identify failure modes in a target foundation model (e.g., an ASR or multimodal system). The critic's outputs are then used to generate sophisticated, black-box adversarial examples that specifically target the discovered weaknesses. These adversarial examples are used to adversarially train the target model, which in turn is evaluated by the continuously updated critic, creating a closed-loop system. This synergy enables automated, iterative hardening of foundation models against complex, semantically meaningful failures that static datasets or simple perturbation methods cannot capture.

ideas.title: Surrogate-Guided Black-Box Attack via Implicit Boundary Learning
ideas.description: This approach challenges the need for an initial transcript by instead constructing a lightweight, local surrogate model from a minimal, adaptive query set. The surrogate learns an implicit approximation of the target model's decision boundary in the region of a given input, providing a search direction. Adversarial candidates are generated using this surrogate and then validated and refined through a consistency-based filtering process against the true black-box oracle, iteratively improving the surrogate's local accuracy and the attack's success rate without gradient estimation or a pre-existing transcript.

ideas.title: Formalize conditions for stable feedback systems
ideas.description: Develop a more rigorous theoretical framework for the stability of model-data feedback loops.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Feedback Loops: Model-driven Amplification of Dataset Biases

ideas.title: Study dataset diversity's role in robustness
ideas.description: Systematically analyze how dataset scale and diversity dimensions confer robustness to natural shifts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Measuring Robustness to Natural Distribution Shifts in Image Classification

ideas.title: Extend SCA prompting to multimodal LLMs
ideas.description: Apply the SCA prompting method to multimodal models to reduce hallucinations in image and video generation tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: "Sorry, Come Again?" Prompting - Enhancing Comprehension and Diminishing Hallucination with [PAUSE]-injected Optimal Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Theoretical Frameworks for Emergent Efficiency: Predicting Capabilities from Algorithmic and Architectural Innovations
ideas.description: This research program seeks to develop a new class of theoretical frameworks that explain and predict emergent capabilities not as a function of compute and data scale alone, but as a function of algorithmic and architectural efficiency. The core hypothesis is that specific innovations in model design, training dynamics, and data utilization can induce phase transitions in capability at drastically reduced computational costs. The goal is to invert the traditional scaling paradigm: instead of scaling up to find emergent abilities, we aim to theoretically identify the minimal efficient pathways to induce them, directly linking the theory of emergence with the practice of sustainable AI. This would provide a principled guide for creating the next generation of high-capability, low-environmental-impact foundation models.

ideas.title: Scale up to multimodal foundation model feedback
ideas.description: Investigate feedback loops for large multimodal models generating text, code, and images for future training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Feedback Loops: Model-driven Amplification of Dataset Biases

ideas.title: Dual: Study prompts that intentionally induce hallucination
ideas.description: Systematically craft prompts to trigger confident hallucinations, creating a diagnostic benchmark.
ideas.sourceTitle: "Sorry, Come Again?" Prompting - Enhancing Comprehension and Diminishing Hallucination with [PAUSE]-injected Optimal Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Carbon-Budget-Constrained Algorithmic Efficiency for Net-Reduction Training
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that couples radical algorithmic efficiency improvements with enforceable carbon accounting to guarantee a net reduction in AI's training footprint. It creates new algorithms designed to operate within strict, verifiable carbon budgets, alongside open-source tooling to audit and allocate these budgets across research and development cycles. The system's core mechanism treats efficiency gains as a multiplier for reducing the allowed carbon expenditure per project, preventing rebound effects. The goal is to shift the industry paradigm from merely improving FLOPS/Watt to absolutely minimizing total emissions, making sustainable training a measurable, non-negotiable requirement.

ideas.title: Transfer active learning from data science
ideas.description: Use active learning strategies to optimize human feedback queries for agent training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction

ideas.title: Applying Microcosm Ecology to Study Feedback in Online Communities
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological solution of using microcosms to study complex feedback loops. In ecology, sealed systems like Biosphere 2 allow for the controlled study of nutrient and behavioral cycles. Adapted to the original seed idea of studying model-data feedback, we propose treating a niche online community or specialized digital domain as an analogous 'digital microcosm.' By instrumenting this bounded, small-scale community to comprehensively log algorithmic outputs (from recommendation engines, AI moderators, or search rankings) and all user-generated data and interactions, researchers can map the cause-and-effect chains of feedback. This setup enables controlled interventions (e.g., A/B testing different algorithm rules) to observe how specific model behaviors amplify, dampen, or distort community dynamics, isolating the internal feedback effects from the noise of the broader internet.

ideas.title: Ensemble Divergence Audit: Breaking Symmetric Blind Spots in Foundation Model Critique
ideas.description: This research develops a critique system that overcomes the inherent symmetry between a monolithic critic model and its target by employing a diverse ensemble of specialized auditors. Each auditor is constrained by design—using different data subsets, architectural priors, or explicit constitutional principles—to force divergent failure modes and perspectives. A meta-controller analyzes the pattern of agreements and disagreements among the ensemble, specifically trained to identify contentious or uncertain judgments that may indicate a systemic blind spot shared by standard models. This framework produces not just a critique, but a map of epistemic uncertainty, directing targeted human oversight to the most likely locations of mutual model failure.

ideas.title: Auditing and Correcting for Curation Bias in Self-Improving Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research investigates methods to detect, quantify, and mitigate the bias introduced when human or algorithmic curators filter model outputs for training. Instead of accepting curated data as an improved ground truth, the system explicitly models the curation process as a separate influence. It employs techniques like propensity scoring and counterfactual data analysis to measure the distributional shift caused by curation, enabling the training of models that are robust to or can correct for this shift. The outcome is a framework for transparently managing curator influence, ensuring the final model's improvements are driven by genuine signal rather than the curator's latent preferences.

ideas.title: Adapting Radioactive Tracer Techniques from Hydrology to Track and Filter Synthetic Data Lineage
ideas.description: This idea adapts the tracer technique from hydrology to the problem of tracking model-generated content in training datasets. In hydrology, tracers are used to quantify the contribution of a specific source to a composite water flow. Analogously, we propose that each AI model or version is assigned a unique, statistically identifiable 'tracer'—a subtle signature embedded in its outputs. By developing detection methods to scan datasets for these tracers, we can move beyond binary classification to precisely quantify the lineage and proportion of synthetic content from specific sources. This enables the creation of filters that can exclude data exceeding a defined threshold of model-generated content, or to re-weight training samples based on their synthetic lineage, mitigating the risks of model collapse and data pollution.

ideas.title: Psychoacoustic-Guided Multi-Image Adversarial Instructions
ideas.description: This research fuses psychoacoustic masking principles with multi-image instruction following to create a novel adversarial attack and defense framework for vision-language models. The core idea is to generate adversarial instructions that require reasoning over a sequence of images, where minimal, perceptually masked perturbations are distributed across the image set. These perturbations are designed using models of visual attention and masking—inspired by audio coding—so they are individually imperceptible but collectively manipulate the model's cross-image reasoning process. This approach tests and potentially improves the robustness of multi-image systems against sophisticated, context-dependent attacks that exploit the temporal and relational nature of sequential visual input.

ideas.title: Incorporate ideas from multi-agent systems
ideas.description: Model the feedback simulation as a multi-agent interaction to capture diverse human perspectives or biases.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: Algorithmic Sparsification via Neuromorphic Pruning for Efficient Training
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where the brain optimizes its energy use and efficiency through developmental synaptic pruning, this research proposes to adapt this biological solution to machine learning training. The analogous solution involves the selective elimination of weak neural connections to streamline the network. The adapted idea is to create novel training algorithms that perform dynamic, in-training sparsification—pruning not just model weights but also computational graph elements—based on their instantaneous salience to the learning objective. This 'neuromorphic pruning' approach would embed efficiency directly into the training process itself, aiming to achieve state-of-the-art model performance with a dramatically reduced environmental footprint by focusing compute resources only where they are most impactful.

ideas.title: Decision-Boundary-Aligned Ensemble for Robust Transfer Attacks
ideas.description: This approach addresses the misalignment problem in substitute model attacks by constructing an ensemble of diverse surrogate models trained via query-efficient model stealing on synthetically generated data. A meta-attack module learns to combine the gradients from these models, prioritizing those with higher consensus and confidence to better approximate the true target model's decision boundary. The system then crafts adversarial examples from this fused, boundary-aligned surrogate, significantly improving transferability across domains. This method is designed to be particularly effective for non-continuous data modalities like text, where gradient matching is traditionally poor.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Tools for AR Model Optimization Inspired by Geophysical Inversion Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research idea proposes creating diagnostic tools for Autoregressive (AR) model optimization by drawing an analogy to reliability diagnostics in geophysical inversion. In geophysics, inversion algorithms estimate subsurface models from indirect data, and a suite of tools—like resolution matrices, L-curves, posterior covariance, and synthetic checkerboard tests—are used to diagnose when the solution is non-unique, unstable, or unreliable. Adapted to AR models, the project would develop analogous computational diagnostics: a sensitivity matrix to trace data influence on the density estimate; controlled synthetic experiments to map systematic recovery failures; and curvature analysis of the optimization landscape to pinpoint ill-posed directions. These tools would help practitioners diagnose when AR model optimization fails to produce a trustworthy density estimate, moving beyond simple loss convergence to a more rigorous assessment of estimation reliability.

ideas.title: Safe Open-Loop Transfer via Meta-Learned Gating for Cross-Task Feedback
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for open-loop, cross-task influence that is robust to negative transfer. It introduces a meta-gating module trained to predict the impact of proposed feedback from a source model/task on a target model's training. This gate selectively permits or blocks feedback based on learned criteria like task similarity and gradient alignment, ensuring only beneficial information flows. The system is co-trained with the primary models, allowing it to adaptively safeguard against performance degradation while enabling positive knowledge transfer.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Diversity Indices to Measure AI Model Portfolio Homogenization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology to the problem of AI model homogenization. In ecology, diversity indices like the Shannon and Simpson indices are standard tools for quantifying species diversity, richness, and evenness to assess ecosystem resilience and vulnerability to monoculture collapse. The adapted research idea applies these same mathematical indices to measure the 'biodiversity' of AI model portfolios. By calculating diversity indices based on model capabilities, architectural features, or training data signatures across a market or research community, we can create a standardized, interpretable metric for model homogenization. This directly quantifies the systemic risk posed by over-concentration on a few model types, framing the health of the AI ecosystem in terms familiar from the study of biological ecosystems.

ideas.title: Evaluating Curated Data Pipelines via Simulator Generalization Stress Tests
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the curation or filtering of scraped data (Idea A) produces training data that is robustly high-quality or merely optimized for a narrow benchmark. It does this by using a broad, complex distribution of instruction-following tasks (Idea B) to stress-test an LLM-based feedback simulator trained on the curated data. The core hypothesis is that if the simulator fails to generalize its feedback judgments to this broader distribution, it signals that the curation process may have created a fragile 'feedback bubble,' optimizing for superficial patterns rather than generalizable principles of quality. This approach turns simulator generalization into a powerful audit for data curation pipelines.

ideas.title: Establish a 'Turing Test Battery' for Foundation Models using principles from clinical neuropsychology
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical neuropsychology, this research idea adapts the solution of using a standardized test battery for comprehensive cognitive assessment. The analogous field solves the problem of evaluating a complex, multi-faceted system (the human brain) by deploying a suite of validated, domain-specific tests that together generate a detailed functional profile. Adapted to foundation models, the idea is to design a 'Turing Test Battery'—a modular benchmark suite where each module acts as a neuropsychological test for a specific AI capability (e.g., logical deduction, semantic coherence, social reasoning, robustness). The solution is not a single metric but a structured methodology to systematically probe, score, and profile a model's capabilities and failure modes across isolated and integrated tasks, providing a diagnostic tool for model development and deployment akin to a clinical assessment.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Model Failures to Dataset Biases via Natural Shifts
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods that use natural shift testbeds as a diagnostic probe for foundation model interpretability. By analyzing how a model's internal representations and decision pathways systematically change when presented with shifted data, we can isolate the specific mechanisms that encode dataset biases. This allows us to move beyond merely measuring performance drops on shifts to causally attributing those failures to particular spurious correlations or under-representations in the original training set. The result is a closed-loop auditing framework where interpretability techniques explain the manifestations of bias, and shift-based measurements ground those explanations in quantifiable dataset properties.

ideas.title: Self-Distilling Hallucination-Aware Specialized Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to create efficient, specialized models by using the large source model itself to generate a training signal for hallucination robustness. Instead of relying on human-labeled hallucination data, the approach analyzes the large model's generation variations, confidence scores, and internal contradiction patterns to automatically categorize error types and severities. This automatically curated 'hallucination awareness' dataset then guides a distillation process that prioritizes factual fidelity and calibration, producing a smaller model that is not just a compressed version but is specifically optimized for reliability within its target domain. The result is a scalable technique for building trustworthy, domain-adapted models without the bottleneck of extensive manual annotation.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Online Preference Learning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a system of multiple specialized agents, each equipped with its own reward model that is updated online based on its interactions and outcomes. Instead of a single fixed reward model, each agent's reward model co-adapts with its policy, allowing it to refine its understanding of preferences within its specific domain of expertise. The agents interact, potentially sharing reward signals or critiques, leading to a decentralized and emergent alignment process where specialization and evaluation mechanisms evolve together. This creates a more robust and adaptable alternative to aligning a single monolithic model with a static human feedback signal.

ideas.title: The Sovereign Compute & Alignment Commons: Decentralizing the Bottlenecks for Competitive and Safe Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research designs a framework that overcomes the resource fragmentation of pure decentralization by creating a sovereign, collectively governed infrastructure for the three key inputs to model development. It establishes a physically decentralized but logically coordinated compute grid, a data trust with governance rights for contributors, and a modular alignment layer where safety and behavioral protocols are developed as open-source, composable modules. This structure allows for distributed contribution and governance while enabling the aggregation of resources necessary to train and maintain models that are both competitively capable and verifiably aligned with the commons' constitutions, directly addressing the coordination and safety failures of naive decentralization.

ideas.title: Neural Metabolic Scaling: Creating Efficient Specialized Models via Biological Resource Allocation Principles
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's metabolic scaling theory, this research adapts the principle of biological specialization through optimized resource allocation to AI model scaling. In nature, generalist organisms evolve into efficient specialists by down-regulating unnecessary metabolic pathways and amplifying those crucial for their niche. Similarly, we propose methods to transform large, general-purpose foundation models into efficient, domain-specific versions not merely by distilling knowledge, but by structurally and dynamically re-allocating computational 'metabolism'. This involves systematically identifying and pruning or freezing less relevant neural components (down-regulation) while intensively training and potentially expanding the critical sub-networks for the target domain (up-regulation). The result is a specialized model architecture with a computational resource profile fundamentally optimized for its specific tasks, achieving greater efficiency than a uniformly scaled-down generalist.

ideas.title: Open-System Stress Testing for AI-Data Feedback in Specialized Communities
ideas.description: This research studies model-data feedback loops by treating niche online communities as open systems subject to external pressures, not closed laboratories. The experimental design introduces simulated real-world complexities, including exogenous information shocks, the deployment of competing AI models with different biases, and measured leakage of AI-influenced content into adjacent communities. This approach tests whether observed feedback dynamics are robust and generalizable, or merely artifacts of an artificially isolated research setting, thereby producing findings more applicable to the deployment of AI in real social ecosystems.

ideas.title: Stabilizing the Stabilizers: Active Meta-Learning for Robust Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
ideas.description: This research investigates how to stabilize the human-in-the-loop component itself within automated feedback systems. It designs an active learning framework that continuously models the reliability, bias, and temporal drift of individual human labelers using sequential Bayesian inference and anomaly detection. The system dynamically allocates labeling tasks to counteract identified instabilities, calibrates labels via an ensemble consensus model, and uses the stabilized human signal to train a robust, uncertainty-aware automated feedback policy. The outcome is a dual-stabilization architecture where both the automated system and the human oversight process are mutually reinforcing and resilient to corruption.

ideas.title: Adversarial Policy Learning for Cross-Modal Domain Alignment
ideas.description: This research frames the challenge of aligning image and text embedding spaces as a reinforcement learning problem, where the agent's policy is to generate adversarial examples. Instead of using domain adaptation in a static, one-way manner, the RL agent learns to produce targeted perturbations that, when applied to inputs from one modality, maximize a reward signal based on improved alignment metrics in a joint embedding space. This enables an adaptive, iterative alignment process that can discover non-obvious, semantically meaningful transformations to bridge the modality gap. Consequently, the method simultaneously learns a robust attack policy that reveals the most critical misalignment points and uses those attacks as a driving force for creating more invariant and generalized multimodal representations.

ideas.title: Focus on interpretable, verifiable decisions over performance
ideas.description: Build agents whose decision-making process is transparent and easily audited.
ideas.sourceTitle: An Interactive Agent Foundation Model

ideas.title: Provenance-Enabled Feedback Loop Analysis in Niche Data Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research fuses data provenance tracking with small-scale feedback analysis to study model-data feedback loops in closed, niche communities like technical forums or specialized subreddits. By meticulously tracking the lineage of every training example, including any model-generated content posted to the community, we can quantify the precise rate and pathways of synthetic data contamination within the ecosystem. This allows for the creation of detailed causal models of feedback dynamics, measuring how output from one model iteration becomes poisonous input for the next. The constrained scale makes exhaustive provenance tracking feasible, turning the community into a living lab for observing and mitigating data degradation in real-time.

ideas.title: Scale down: efficient small CLIP models
ideas.description: Design highly efficient CLIP variants for deployment where captions are scarce or compute is limited.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is a Caption Worth a Thousand Images? A Controlled Study for Representation Learning

ideas.title: Preventive Ecosystem Engineering for Foundation Model Diversity
ideas.description: This research designs technical standards and economic mechanisms to actively prevent the emergence of a single-model monoculture. It creates open interoperability protocols that allow applications to dynamically compose workflows across multiple, heterogeneous foundation models, reducing lock-in and systemic risk. The work includes developing efficient cross-model routing, adaptation layer ensembles, and incentive models that reward robust, specialized model development. The goal is to make a diverse, resilient model ecosystem more economically attractive and technically seamless than reliance on any single provider.

ideas.title: Circuit Analysis for Foundation Models via Perturbation and Causal Tracing
ideas.description: This research adapts the classic systems neuroscience approach of lesion studies combined with causal pathway tracing to the domain of foundation model interpretability. In neuroscience, function is mapped by perturbing specific brain regions and tracing the propagation of signals through neural circuits. The adapted idea is to create a suite of tools that algorithmically perform analogous targeted perturbations (e.g., ablating attention heads, clamping activations) and causal tracing (e.g., propagating modified input representations, tracking gradient flows) within large, pre-trained models. The objective is to move beyond saliency maps and feature visualization to discover and functionally characterize the internal, often distributed, 'circuits'—reusable computational sub-networks—that implement specific reasoning steps, factual recalls, or biases, thereby building a mechanistic map of the model's decision-making processes.

ideas.title: Societal Risk from the Robustness Gap: Auditing Foundational Models for Real-World Distribution Shifts
ideas.description: This research investigates how the failure of synthetically-trained robustness to transfer to natural distribution shifts in foundation models creates specific, predictable societal risks. It moves beyond technical diagnosis to model how these robustness gaps, when deployed at scale, lead to cascading failures in economic systems (e.g., biased credit assessments during market shifts), legal inequities (e.g., inconsistent judicial aid across demographic subgroups), and ethical harms. The outcome is an interdisciplinary audit methodology that maps technical robustness shortcomings to probable real-world impact scenarios, enabling proactive governance and more informed deployment policies for high-stakes applications.

ideas.title: Factual Stability in Multi-Model Feedback Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research investigates the conditions for maximal factual fidelity within a dynamic ecosystem where multiple AI models generate content that is mixed into future training data. It maps how prompts and contexts that induce high accuracy in individual models interact through feedback loops, either reinforcing a shared factual basin or triggering error cascades. The core novelty is identifying topological and dynamical 'attractors' for truth within the ecosystem's state space, rather than within a single model. This provides a framework for designing multi-model training and deployment protocols that are robust to factual drift over successive generations.

ideas.title: Apply Drug Discovery's Surrogate Model & Acquisition Function to Black-Box Attacks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core Bayesian optimization framework from computational drug discovery to the problem of query-efficient black-box adversarial attacks. In drug discovery, Bayesian optimization is used to minimize expensive lab tests by using a surrogate model to predict molecular activity and an acquisition function to guide the next experiment. Analogously, in a black-box attack, each query to the target model is expensive (risking detection).
