We adapt the solution by building a surrogate model (e.g., a Gaussian Process or a neural network) that learns the mapping from perturbed inputs to the target model's output (e.g., loss relative to the true class). An acquisition function (like Expected Improvement or Upper Confidence Bound) is then used to select the next input to query, explicitly trading off exploring uncertain regions of the input space and exploiting areas predicted to cause high loss. This formal, guided search process directly minimizes the number of queries needed to craft a successful adversarial example, transferring the efficient experimental design paradigm from drug discovery to machine learning security.

ideas.title: Bayesian Optimization for Query-Efficient Black-Box Attacks with Generalizable Simulator Validation
ideas.description: This research integrates Bayesian optimization (BO) into query-efficient black-box attacks against LLMs, but uses the attack's need for diverse queries to systematically validate an LLM-based simulator's generalization. The BO algorithm's acquisition function will be designed to strategically select attack queries that are maximally informative for both optimizing the attack and stress-testing the simulator on a broad, complex distribution of instruction-following tasks. This creates a dual-purpose loop where each attack query also serves as a validation point, revealing whether simulator feedback remains reliable as attack strategies and task complexity evolve. Consequently, the work simultaneously advances query-efficient attack methods and provides a rigorous, task-driven evaluation framework for the generalizability of LLM simulators.

ideas.title: Bayesian Optimization for Data Reconstruction in RLHF-Tuned Models
ideas.description: This research proposes using Bayesian optimization (BO) as an active inference tool to solve the inverse problem of reconstructing training data from foundation models fine-tuned with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Instead of tuning RLHF hyperparameters, BO is used to efficiently search the high-dimensional input space for candidate data samples that, when presented to the model, elicit target behaviors or internal activations indicative of memorized or influential training points. This transforms the data reconstruction challenge into a sequential optimization problem, allowing for the targeted extraction of sensitive data from complex, black-box RLHF pipelines. The synergy enables a principled, query-efficient attack that can uncover specific data artifacts responsible for a model's aligned behavior, assessing privacy risks post-alignment.

ideas.title: Program Synthesis for Fairness-Aware Data Generation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates algorithmic fairness interventions directly into the program synthesis process used for evaluation. Instead of merely generating code to test outputs, the system synthesizes fairness-aware generators or agents whose core logic is constrained by formal, executable fairness specifications from the outset. This pre-emptively shapes the behavior and data produced by these synthesized programs, ensuring they adhere to defined equity metrics before they ever interact with a broader data pool or environment. The synergy enables the creation of self-regulating, verifiably fair synthetic data pipelines and autonomous agents, moving bias mitigation from a post-hoc filter to a foundational design principle enforced by construction.

ideas.title: Active Forensic Detection and Attribution of Model-Generated Content Without Provenance Reliance
ideas.description: This system addresses the unreliability of provenance metadata by developing a forensic detector that identifies synthetic content directly from the data itself. It trains a classifier on a diverse corpus of known human- and model-generated content across modalities, learning to recognize subtle artifacts and statistical signatures characteristic of generative models. An active verification module applies targeted transformations and consistency probes to samples, amplifying detectable signals of machine generation. The output is a confidence score and likely source model family for each data point, enabling filtering and reweighting of datasets even when lineage records are missing, corrupted, or adversarial.

ideas.title: Proactive Governance Platform for Simulating and Stress-Testing Foundation Model Deployment Trajectories
ideas.description: This research establishes a dynamic, computational platform that integrates agent-based economic models, legal graph analysis, and participatory scenario workshops to simulate long-term societal outcomes under different deployment and regulatory pathways. It focuses on identifying critical intervention points and policy levers *before* lock-in occurs, by stress-testing proposed governance frameworks against a range of adversarial and unintended consequence scenarios. The output is a continuously updated set of evidence-based, contingent policy playbooks designed for regulators and industry, moving beyond description to enable proactive governance of foundation model ecosystems.

ideas.title: Applying Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) from Systems Engineering to Foundation Model Security
ideas.description: This research adapts the Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) methodology from Systems and Reliability Engineering to the domain of foundation model security. In its original field, FMEA provides a systematic, proactive framework for identifying potential failure points in a complex system, analyzing their causes and effects, and prioritizing risks based on severity, likelihood, and detectability. The adapted idea applies this structured approach to foundation models. Researchers would develop a security-focused FMEA framework to catalog novel attack surfaces (e.g., vulnerabilities in the training pipeline, inference API, or deployed application), trace the causes and potential cascading effects of adversarial exploits, and assign a quantifiable risk priority. This methodical process enables the systematic identification and mitigation of security threats before they are exploited, shifting the paradigm from reactive cybersecurity to proactive, design-for-security in AI development.

ideas.title: Strategic Human Sampling for Stabilizing Unstable Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research adapts the control theory concept of 'gain scheduling' to the problem of stabilizing unstable automated feedback systems in AI training. In aerospace engineering, gain scheduling stabilizes aircraft by switching control parameters based on flight conditions. The analogous solution is to treat the AI training process as a dynamical system and design a human-labeling scheduler. This scheduler would monitor real-time stability metrics (e.g., gradient explosion, reward divergence, or loss of confidence) and strategically inject human-labeled data at precisely defined operational 'regimes' or instability thresholds. The adapted idea studies how this strategic, condition-based human intervention can correct and stabilize the feedback loop, enabling more robust and scalable training while minimizing the need for continuous human oversight.

ideas.title: Phase Transition Theory for Emergent Capabilities in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the theoretical framework of phase transitions from statistical physics to predict and explain emergent capabilities in foundation models. In physics, phase transition theory successfully predicts how new macroscopic properties (like magnetization) emerge in many-body systems at critical points as control parameters (e.g., temperature) are scaled. The analogous solution involves identifying order parameters and scaling laws. Adapted to AI, we propose to treat model scale (parameters, data, compute) as the control parameter and define quantitative 'capability order parameters' for tasks like reasoning or coding. The goal is to create a theoretical framework that predicts critical scaling thresholds where qualitatively new capabilities emerge, identifying universal scaling exponents and constructing capability phase diagrams to explain and forecast emergent phenomena.

ideas.title: Task-Dependent Stability Analysis of LLM Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research develops a formal stability theory for LLM-in-the-loop systems where the feedback dynamics are conditioned on the specific task being performed. It posits that the stability of a model-data feedback loop is not a fixed property of the model, but a function of the task formulation, which dictates the latent structure of potential hallucinations. The core theoretical challenge is to derive formal conditions—likely involving task-specific attractor basins in the output space—under which iterative generation or refinement will converge to a truthful region or diverge into a hallucinatory one. This enables the design of task-aware feedback controllers and training protocols that actively steer systems away from unstable, hallucination-prone operational modes.

ideas.title: The Scale-Alternative Benchmark: A Controlled Test for Achieving Scaled Capabilities with Sub-Linear Compute
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous, falsifiable benchmark to test whether targeted algorithmic interventions can achieve capabilities comparable to those from simple scaling laws. It involves training paired model families—one via standard scaling and one under a strict compute budget but with enhanced training methods—from identical initializations. Success is quantitatively defined as the alternative model meeting a pre-defined performance threshold (e.g., 90%) across a broad evaluation suite relative to its scaled counterpart. The outcome provides a definitive, empirical answer to whether scale is the only path to broad capability.

ideas.title: Ecologically Valid Adversarial Attacks for Adaptive Embedded AI Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for generating adversarial examples that are both computationally efficient and effective against real-world, adaptively defended embedded AI systems. Instead of just minimizing computational footprint, the approach explicitly models and overcomes common embedded defense mechanisms—such as temporal smoothing, quantization-aware filtering, and anomaly detectors—by treating them as part of the optimization environment. The resulting attacks are validated on a benchmark of diverse low-power hardware platforms under realistic sensor noise and latency constraints, providing a true measure of physical-world feasibility against adaptive adversaries. This ensures demonstrated vulnerabilities are not merely artifacts of simplified laboratory conditions but reflect credible threats to deployed systems.

ideas.title: Genomic Forensics: Reconstructing Ancestral Populations from Modern Genetic Signatures
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem from population genetics, where ancestral populations are reconstructed from modern genetic signatures, to the domain of machine learning. The analogous field is Population Genetics and Phylogenetics, where solutions use statistical models (like coalescent theory) on present-day genetic variation to infer historical parameters such as population size, mixture proportions, and divergence events. The adapted research idea treats a finalized foundation model as the 'modern population' and aims to deduce statistical properties and potential exemplars of its 'ancestral' training data. By developing inverse optimization and statistical inference techniques on the model's weight distributions, activation patterns, and output behaviors, the research will investigate the feasibility of reconstructing data distribution characteristics, source mixture ratios, and identifying signatures of highly influential or memorized training samples, addressing the scaling duality of model behavior and its data origins.

ideas.title: Co-Evolutionary Meta-Dynamics for Non-Stationary Data Ecosystems
ideas.description: This framework models competing data distributions not as fixed species, but as entities capable of strategic adaptation within a feedback loop. It combines population dynamics with co-evolutionary game theory and meta-learning, where the 'interaction rules' between distributions are themselves shaped by the performance outcomes of prior interactions. The system simulates long-term outcomes by jointly tracking the state of the data environment and the learning trajectories of the agents (e.g., recommendation algorithms, content creators) that generate the data, predicting regime shifts and potential collapse into low-diversity states.

ideas.title: Decentralized Model Governance with Multi-Dimensional Performance Audits
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized framework for developing and governing foundation models where control is distributed, but the governance mechanism itself is driven by a sophisticated, multi-dimensional audit of model performance. Instead of relying on average accuracy to guide decisions, the framework mandates continuous evaluation using a suite of metrics including calibration, worst-group robustness, and fairness across participant-defined subgroups. This creates a closed-loop system where decentralized stakeholders can propose, vote on, and implement model updates, but their success is measured and ranked by how they improve these nuanced performance dimensions for all represented groups, not just the majority. The synergy transforms decentralization from a purely structural change into a performance-driven ecosystem that inherently optimizes for equitable and reliable AI.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Foundation Models via Synthetic Data Ecosystems, Inspired by Directed Evolution
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of training on broad, general data by proposing foundation models trained on deep, curated, or synthetic data for specific domains. Drawing an analogy from protein engineering, where directed evolution bypasses the search through all natural proteins to rapidly engineer enzymes for specific tasks, we adapt its core principles. The analogous solution involves creating a focused, synthetic library of gene variants and using iterative selection. Adapted to AI, this becomes a 'directed data evolution' methodology: start with a curated seed of domain truth, use a model to generate a vast, principled synthetic dataset encompassing the domain's nuance and complexity, and train the foundation model from scratch on this ecosystem. Iterative rounds of generation and evaluation, guided by domain-specific performance metrics, would evolve the model's proficiency, potentially yielding more reliable, interpretable, and data-efficient domain experts than models derived from broad pre-training.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Stabilization of Multi-Sensory Data Feedback Loops for Embodied Agents
ideas.description: This research direction applies control theory to model and stabilize the closed-loop dynamics between an embodied agent and its environment when the agent's actions are informed by a continuous, multi-sensory data stream. It moves beyond passive fusion to actively design feedback controllers that regulate how audio, haptic, proprioceptive, and visual data are weighted, integrated, and used for decision-making over time. This ensures the agent's internal world model remains consistent and actionable despite sensory noise, delays, or conflicts, enabling robust operation in complex real-world settings where sensory feedback loops are critical.

ideas.title: Human-Guided Knowledge Graph Curation for Stable Agent Reasoning
ideas.description: This research investigates a dual human-AI system where an autonomous agent performs tasks using reasoning via traversal of a large-scale knowledge graph. The key innovation is using strategic human-in-the-loop labeling not on the agent's final outputs, but on the graph traversal paths and reasoning steps the agent uses. Humans identify unstable, contradictory, or unreliable reasoning chains within the graph structure and provide corrective labels or constraints. These human corrections are then used to dynamically stabilize the knowledge graph itself and train the agent's reasoning policy, creating a self-improving cycle where the graph becomes a verified, stability-enhancing resource for autonomous systems.

ideas.title: Dynamic Stress-Testing and Causal Attribution for Model Homogenization Risk
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static econometric measures by developing a framework that actively probes for latent homogenization through dynamic stress-testing. It subjects a population of AI models to a battery of targeted, OOD challenges and adversarial inputs to measure correlated failures and systemic fragility. The framework then employs causal discovery methods on model architectures, training data fingerprints, and learning trajectories to attribute these correlated failures to specific, shared root causes. This produces not just a homogenization score, but a diagnostic profile that identifies actionable intervention points to reduce systemic risk.

ideas.title: Perceptual Constraint-Based Audio Adversarial Crafting
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from medical image reconstruction, where diagnostic quality is preserved during noisy signal recovery by balancing data fidelity with perceptual regularization. The analogous problem in audio adversarial attacks is preserving original audio quality while achieving misclassification. The adapted research idea is to develop adversarial crafting techniques that use a perceptually-informed optimization framework. This framework would incorporate a psychoacoustic model (e.g., based on auditory masking thresholds) as a constraint or regularization term, directly shaping the adversarial perturbation to reside in perceptual 'null spaces,' thereby enhancing audio similarity during attacks.

ideas.title: Directed Dataset Evolution via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of 'succession management' to machine learning dataset curation. The core assumption challenged is that a dataset must be statically curated or continuously stabilized. The analogous solution from ecology involves guiding an ecosystem through a controlled sequence of intermediate states (pioneer, intermediate, climax communities) to reach a desired endpoint. Adapted here, the research idea is to design feedback mechanisms that do not aim for dataset stability but for a controlled, multi-phase evolution. A controller would iteratively introduce specific 'pioneer' data batches or apply targeted biases (e.g., altering class ratios, feature correlations). These pioneers are chosen based on how they shift the dataset's properties to create the conditions necessary for the successful introduction of the next, more advanced data batch, gradually and reliably steering the overall dataset distribution toward a predefined target, much like a restoration ecologist steers a landscape.

ideas.title: Dual problem: texture compression via synthesis
ideas.description: Compress textures by storing only a small seed and synthesis parameters.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transposer: Universal Texture Synthesis Using Feature Maps as Transposed Convolution Filter

ideas.title: Causal Stress-Testing for AR Model Density Utility
ideas.description: This research designs controlled experiments to causally evaluate when and why autoregressive model density estimates are useful for downstream tasks. It creates synthetic and real-world task families with known data-generating processes and introduces targeted interventions that selectively degrade the quality or calibration of the model's density estimates. By measuring if downstream performance degrades in precise accordance with theoretical predictions under these interventions, the work moves beyond correlation to establish causal, generalizable principles for density utility. The outcome is a diagnostic framework that identifies the necessary task structures and model properties for reliable density-based reasoning.

ideas.title: Use meta-learning for fast domain adaptation
ideas.description: Incorporate meta-learning to quickly adapt the foundation model to new tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: An Interactive Agent Foundation Model

ideas.title: Scale up simulator to massive instruction diversity
ideas.description: Generate orders of magnitude more diverse synthetic instructions and feedback to stress-test learning algorithms.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: Challenge independent domain sampling
ideas.description: Model and exploit correlations between domains during the data acquisition process.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Multi-Domain Active Learning on Image Classification

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trophic Filters to Model-Generated Training Data
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that scraped model outputs should be a primary feedback source by drawing an analogy from ecological nutrient cycling. In ecosystems, decomposers and detritivores filter and transform biomass, preventing toxin accumulation and recycling nutrients in a usable form. The adapted research idea proposes creating an analogous 'data trophic layer' for AI training pipelines. This layer would consist of intermediate systems (specialized models, human curation protocols, or validation ensembles) tasked not with simple selection, but with active decomposition and transformation of raw model outputs scraped from the wild. Their goal is to break down coherent but incorrect information, neutralize amplified biases, and convert the raw data into a refined, validated, and pedagogically structured form of feedback. This process aims to prevent the accumulation of 'data toxins' in the training loop, promoting a more stable and reliable learning cycle for the primary model, much like decomposers maintain the health of an ecosystem.

ideas.title: Distributed Micro-Attacks via Swarm Intelligence Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts swarm intelligence principles from robotics to demonstrate physically feasible attacks on low-resource hardware. The analogous solution from swarm robotics shows how complex global behaviors can emerge from many simple agents following minimal local rules. The adapted research idea is to develop efficient adversarial algorithms where the 'attack' is distributed across a swarm of simple, embedded nodes. Each node runs an extremely lightweight process, requiring minimal memory and CPU, but through coordinated action based on principles like ant colony optimization or flocking rules, the swarm collectively executes a sophisticated physical-world attack, proving feasibility on hardware far below typical computational thresholds.

ideas.title: Systematic Validation of AR Density Estimates via Benchmarking Protocols from Medical Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the rigorous validation protocols used in medical diagnostics for biomarkers to the problem of identifying where AR model density estimates are reliable. In the analogous field, a new diagnostic test is not assumed to be universally useful; instead, it is validated against a gold standard in specific clinical contexts to determine its sensitivity, specificity, and predictive value. Adapted to ML, the solution is to create a systematic benchmarking framework. First, establish task categories (e.g., out-of-distribution detection, uncertainty quantification for sequential prediction) and define verifiable 'gold standard' probability measures for each, possibly using massive held-out datasets or controlled synthetic data environments. Then, rigorously evaluate AR density estimates against these standards across a wide array of models and datasets, quantifying their calibration, discriminative power, and robustness. The outcome is a diagnostic map that clearly identifies the task types, data regimes, and model architectures where AR density serves as a reliable proxy for true likelihood, thereby guiding its trustworthy application in downstream tasks.

ideas.title: Challenge LLM-simulated feedback as human proxy
ideas.description: Investigate systematic biases where LLM-simulated feedback diverges from human preferences, especially on subjective or creative tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: Enable real-time interactive learning and adaptation
ideas.description: Develop methods for the agent to learn continuously from online interaction.
ideas.sourceTitle: An Interactive Agent Foundation Model

ideas.title: Use Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter search
ideas.description: Apply Bayesian optimization to efficiently tune the many hyperparameters of RLHF methods within the simulator.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: Transfer domain adaptation to modality gap
ideas.description: Use domain adaptation methods to align the image and text embedding spaces more effectively.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is a Caption Worth a Thousand Images? A Controlled Study for Representation Learning

ideas.title: Adopt game theory for selection
ideas.description: Frame multi-domain selection as a game between domains to optimize global utility.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Multi-Domain Active Learning on Image Classification

ideas.title: Automated High-Throughput Phenotyping for AI Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from plant genomics—high-throughput phenotyping platforms (HTPPs)—to the challenge of scaling AI evaluation. In agriculture, HTPPs use automation, sensor arrays, and controlled environments to assess thousands of plant traits rapidly across vast populations and conditions, moving biology from small-scale manual observation to industrial-scale measurement. Adapted here, the research proposes building an 'AI Phenotyping' framework. This involves creating a distributed, automated testbed where lightweight evaluation 'probes' are deployed en masse to interact with AI models across a combinatorially vast space of scenarios (simulated environments, edge-case inputs, adversarial conditions, and real-world API interactions). The system would automatically measure and catalog a model's multi-dimensional 'behavioral phenotype'—including performance, robustness, biases, and novel failure modes—generating a comprehensive, scalable evaluation map that grows in complexity alongside the models themselves, ensuring assessment scales in tandem with capability.

ideas.title: Dual: Active domain discovery
ideas.description: The inverse problem: actively select data to best identify or define the latent domains.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Multi-Domain Active Learning on Image Classification

ideas.title: Robust Control for Reflexive Data-Model Systems via Causal-Game Theoretic Integration
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that merges control theory with causal inference and game theory to design stable, performant data feedback loops. It explicitly models the strategic and adaptive behaviors of data-generating agents as part of the system dynamics, moving beyond a passive plant assumption. The approach designs controllers that are robust to a bounded set of these adaptations and incorporates online meta-learning to adjust control policies in response to observed non-stationarity. This results in systems that can provably maintain desired performance and avoid harmful runaway feedback, even when the model's own predictions alter the environment it seeks to measure.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Stability Guardians for Model-Data Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research develops a practical framework for ensuring the operational stability of learning systems in model-data feedback loops, where formal guarantees are often unattainable. Instead of purely analytical conditions, it designs algorithmic 'stability guardians'—runtime monitors that track empirical signals of instability, such as exploding loss gradients or distributional shift metrics. These guardians are integrated with a meta-control layer that can execute stability-preserving interventions, like temporarily decoupling the feedback loop or reverting to a prior stable model. The outcome is a robust, implementable system for maintaining reliable long-term operation of adaptive ML systems in real-world deployments.

ideas.title: Test correlation on more diverse distribution shifts
ideas.description: Empirically validate the strong correlation across a broader set of realistic, complex distribution shifts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Accuracy on the Line: on the Strong Correlation Between Out-of-Distribution and In-Distribution Generalization

ideas.title: Adversarial Scenario Discovery for High-Stakes Evaluation
ideas.description: This framework combines scalable automated red-teaming with structured human expertise to discover and formalize a model's most critical weaknesses. Instead of purely generating countless tests, the system uses automated probes to identify performance boundaries and pockets of overconfidence. These targets are then used to guide a diverse human panel—including domain experts, ethicists, and creative writers—in authoring high-quality, edge-case scenarios. The result is a continuously evolving, high-signal evaluation suite that scales in sophistication and stakes, ensuring evaluation depth keeps pace with model capabilities.

ideas.title: Quantifying Strategic Homogenization in Multi-Modal, Interactive AI Systems
ideas.description: This research fuses econometric concentration indices with dynamic, interactive benchmarking to quantify the risk of strategic homogenization in multi-modal AI systems. It develops a benchmark where models engage in extended, goal-oriented dialogues requiring visual reasoning, and applies measures like the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index to the distribution of their solution strategies and internal decision pathways. This reveals whether diverse models functionally converge when solving complex, interactive tasks, providing a critical metric for ecosystem resilience beyond mere output similarity. The result is a diagnostic tool that assesses both capability and the diversity of reasoning in real-world, interactive AI applications.

ideas.title: Incorporate richer feedback types beyond pairwise
ideas.description: Simulate other human feedback modalities like rankings, numerical scores, or textual corrections within the AlpacaFarm framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: Relax assumption that foundation models are best building blocks
ideas.description: Explore agent architectures not primarily built on existing large foundation models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction

ideas.title: A Modular, Multi-Model Architecture with a Shared Communication Protocol
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of a single monolithic foundation model by proposing a system architecture inspired by cellular signaling in biology. In complex organisms, specialized cell types communicate using a diverse yet standardized chemical language (e.g., neurotransmitters, hormones), enabling coordinated function without a central homogenous controller. Adapted to AI, the solution is to design a system of multiple, specialized models (for perception, reasoning, action, etc.) that interact via a shared, structured communication protocol—a defined set of interpretable message types and formats. This modular approach allows for independent development and scaling of components, easier diagnosis of failures, and dynamic composition for different tasks, moving beyond the limitations of a single homogenized model.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Co-Evolutionary Framework for Longitudinal Human-AI Interaction Research
ideas.description: This research program establishes a continuous, closed-loop system to study the dynamic co-evolution of human trust and AI systems. It combines longitudinal panel studies, experience sampling, and adaptive A/B testing to track how interaction patterns shift in response to deliberate changes in AI behavior and interface design. The framework explicitly models feedback loops, where human adaptation influences subsequent AI design iterations, moving beyond static snapshots. This produces robust, temporal models of trust calibration that can inform the responsible development of adaptive AI technologies.

ideas.title: Eco-Fair Training: Co-designing Efficiency and Fairness via Algorithmic Data Shaping
ideas.description: This research direction posits that the imperative for radical compute reduction can be a powerful lens for developing more effective and fundamental fairness interventions. Instead of treating fairness as a separate, computationally expensive add-on, we propose to co-design novel training algorithms where the constraints of extreme efficiency (e.g., through dynamic data selection, sparse updates, or compact model architectures) force the explicit identification and preservation of fairness-critical signals in the data. This approach aims to pre-emptively shape model behavior by algorithmically curating and weighting training data streams to maximize both fairness and learning efficiency from the outset, potentially leading to models that are not only greener but also more equitable by construction, as their limited capacity is strategically allocated to learn robust, unbiased patterns.

ideas.title: Improve long-horizon reasoning and planning
ideas.description: Enhance the agent's capability for complex, multi-step reasoning and task planning.
ideas.sourceTitle: An Interactive Agent Foundation Model

ideas.title: Modeling Preference Drift via Population Ecology Dynamics
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of a static human preference distribution in AI training and alignment. By drawing an analogy from Population Ecology, specifically predator-prey dynamics, we adapt the solution of using coupled differential equations (like the Lotka-Volterra model) to the AI context. The analogous solution from ecology models co-evolving populations with shifting distributions due to feedback loops. The adapted idea is to model the AI's output distribution and the human preference distribution as two interacting 'species,' formalizing their co-evolution with parameters for exposure, adaptation, and saturation. This dynamical systems approach allows for simulating how preferences drift independently over time due to the AI's own influence, providing a framework to study stability, cycles, and equilibria in long-term AI-human feedback loops.

ideas.title: Challenge need for separate regular/irregular pathways
ideas.description: Develop a unified input mechanism instead of switching between self-similarity and noise maps.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transposer: Universal Texture Synthesis Using Feature Maps as Transposed Convolution Filter

ideas.title: A Risk-Performance Pareto Frontier Analysis of Model Adaptation Paradigms
ideas.description: This research constructs a standardized testbed to empirically map the risk-performance trade-off space for transfer learning, prompting, and retrieval-augmented adaptation. It defines measurable axes for safety risks (e.g., unintended capability generalization, data leakage susceptibility) and performance metrics (adaptation speed, accuracy, compute cost) across diverse tasks. The study systematically varies task difficulty, distribution shift magnitude, and available data to identify the conditions where each paradigm dominates on a Pareto frontier. The outcome is a concrete, evidence-based decision framework for selecting adaptation methods based on quantifiable risk tolerance and resource constraints.

ideas.title: Critic-Guided Dataset Evolution for Foundational Model Steering
ideas.description: This research direction develops a system where a foundation model is paired with a specialized 'critic' model trained to audit its outputs against a multifaceted target specification (e.g., safety, reasoning style, factuality). The key innovation is that the critic's failure analyses are not just for evaluation but are algorithmically inverted into actionable directives to steer a data curation engine. This engine then continuously and intentionally evolves the primary model's training dataset, shifting its distribution to systematically correct the identified deficiencies. Thus, the target model is guided through a controlled evolutionary trajectory, with the critic providing the necessary feedback to turn open-ended dataset evolution into a precise alignment mechanism.

ideas.title: Extend controlled study to other modalities
ideas.description: Apply similar controlled methodology to compare audio-only vs. text-supervised audio representation learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is a Caption Worth a Thousand Images? A Controlled Study for Representation Learning

ideas.title: Tamper-Evident, Cryptographically-Verifiable Provenance Chains for Adversarial Model Auditing
ideas.description: This system designs and implements a secure, decentralized ledger (or similar immutable data structure) that automatically logs cryptographically signed hashes of training data batches, model checkpoints, and inference requests/responses during model operation. The protocol is integrated at the infrastructure level, making omission or alteration detectable by external auditors even if the model operator is non-cooperative. This creates an independently verifiable, time-stamped evidentiary record of the model's data lineage and decision history, enabling robust audits for compliance, liability attribution, and detecting data poisoning or model theft without relying on the goodwill of the audited party.

ideas.title: Analyzing AR Optimization Landscapes via Statistical Physics Methods
ideas.description: This research extends the theoretical analysis of AR optimization landscapes by drawing an analogy from statistical physics, specifically the study of spin glasses. The analogous problem—characterizing complex energy landscapes in disordered systems with many metastable states—was solved using replica theory, TAP equations, and cavity methods. By adapting these techniques, we propose to treat the parameters of complex autoregressive architectures (e.g., Transformers, LSTMs) as interacting spins and the training data as a source of quenched disorder. This approach would allow a rigorous theoretical investigation of the loss landscape geometry, including the distribution of critical points, the structure of saddle points, and the connectivity between different minima, moving beyond simple toy models to realistic deep learning architectures.

ideas.title: Dynamics of Non-Gaussian Preference Distributions in Learning Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-evolutionary dynamics between machine learning systems and the non-Gaussian, complex distributions of human preferences they are trained on. It develops theoretical and empirical models where the data distribution is not static and its covariance structure is not simplistic, allowing for heavy tails, clusters, and skew that better reflect real-world human populations. The core inquiry is to understand how feedback loops—where model outputs influence future human-generated data—cause these rich, non-Gaussian distributions to shift in predictable or chaotic ways over time. This framework aims to reveal stability conditions, polarization mechanisms, and emergent societal patterns that are invisible to analyses relying on static preferences or Gaussian approximations.

ideas.title: Ecological Resilience-Informed Governance for Foundation Model Societies
ideas.description: This research program applies ecological resilience theory—concepts like keystone species, regime shifts, and functional redundancy—to model and govern the long-term societal impacts of foundation model deployment. It moves beyond studying isolated consequences to analyze the complex, adaptive socio-technical system formed by the interaction of AI models, economic structures, legal frameworks, and human communities. The goal is to develop governance mechanisms and system architectures that enhance the overall resilience of this coupled system, preventing catastrophic societal 'phase shifts' and enabling adaptive recovery from unforeseen shocks, thereby ensuring long-term stability and beneficial co-evolution of AI and society.

ideas.title: Online Test-Time Augmentation for Adaptive Calibration in Live Web-Scale Pipelines
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of test-time augmentation (TTA) from medical imaging—where multiple transformed versions of an input are used to stabilize predictions—to the dynamic control plane of a live, web-scale data pipeline. Instead of augmenting static images, the system applies lightweight, plausible perturbations (e.g., to sampling rates, feature subsets, or temporal windows) to the live data stream itself, creating an ensemble of pipeline states. The variance in the outputs of these states provides a real-time, data-driven estimate of pipeline stability and model calibration under natural shift. This enables the continuous, automated application of calibration interventions precisely when and where the pipeline's uncertainty indicates degradation, moving beyond scheduled tests to adaptive, self-correcting data infrastructure.

ideas.title: Federated Model Assembly via Open-Source Biological Protocol Standards
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the centralization of model development and control by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology's solution to decentralized biological engineering. The analogous field is Synthetic Biology, specifically the Open-Source BioBrick Assembly standard pioneered by the iGEM competition and the BioBricks Foundation. Their solution involved creating a global repository of standardized, well-characterized genetic parts with defined functional interfaces and universal assembly rules, enabling distributed teams to innovate by combining these interoperable components. Adapted to foundation model research, this proposes a framework for 'Neural BioBricks': a system of certified, modular neural network components (e.g., transformer blocks, multimodal encoders, reasoning modules) with strict architectural and data interface specifications. An open registry and protocol would allow globally distributed contributors to develop, train on specialized data, and vet individual modules. These modules could then be compositionally assembled by any party following the protocol, enabling the creation of large, capable foundation models through decentralized contribution and governance, rather than monolithic, centrally controlled training. This challenges the core assumption that foundation model scale and capability require centralized compute and control.

ideas.title: Establish a Long-Term Observational and Modeling Framework for Foundation Model Impacts
ideas.description: This research adapts the longitudinal and surveillance methodologies from epidemiology to study the long-term societal impacts of foundation models. Just as public health tracks cohorts exposed to a substance and monitors population-wide health indicators, this project would establish long-term observational cohorts (economic, professional, demographic) and implement continuous surveillance of key societal metrics (job market shifts, legal case outcomes, ethical discourse). The interdisciplinary approach would combine this data collection with advanced causal modeling from economics and sociology to disentangle the effects of foundation model deployment from other technological and social forces, providing a robust, evidence-based foundation for policy and ethical guidance.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Environment predicting agent actions

ideas.title: Apply Geophysical Survey Sampling for Efficient NES Gradient Estimation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysical seismic exploration to improve the efficiency of Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) for black-box gradient estimation. In geophysics, the properties of hidden subsurface structures are inferred using strategically placed sensors and active sources, with inversion techniques optimizing information gain per expensive sample. Adapted to NES, the parameter space is treated as a hidden landscape, and the algorithm's sampling distribution is dynamically optimized: perturbation directions (analogous to sensor placements) are chosen not randomly but to maximize the information about the gradient per fitness evaluation. This involves using the history of samples to build a model of gradient uncertainty and focusing subsequent perturbations to reduce this uncertainty, thereby achieving more reliable gradient estimates with fewer costly black-box function evaluations.

ideas.title: Ecosystemic Discovery: Auditing AI Resilience with Legal-Ecological Frameworks
ideas.description: This research synthesizes legal discovery procedures with ecological resilience theory to develop a novel audit framework for AI systems. It treats the model, its training data, and operational environment as an interconnected ecosystem, applying ecological concepts like redundancy, keystone features, and regime shifts to identify critical vulnerabilities and adaptive pathways. The legal discovery toolkit provides a rigorous, standardized process for evidence collection, documentation, and causal attribution of failures or robustness breaches across this ecosystem. The result is a forensic audit protocol that not only explains past model decisions but also proactively assesses and certifies a system's capacity to maintain function and integrity through distribution shifts and adversarial pressures.

ideas.title: Objective-Driven Scaling Laws: Characterizing Capability Emergence via Loss Landscape Engineering
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the scaling laws governing capability emergence are not universal but are instead a function of the specific training objective. We hypothesize that alternative loss functions and regularization schemes can fundamentally alter the relationship between model size, data volume, and performance, potentially achieving broad capability at significantly reduced scale. The core methodology involves a large-scale empirical search across objective space, measuring the resulting 'scaling law coefficients' for different objectives on standardized capability benchmarks. The outcome would be a predictive map showing which training objectives compress the scaling curves, enabling the principled design of high-performance models that do not rely solely on massive scale.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of single-scale feature sufficiency
ideas.description: Investigate if multi-scale encoded feature maps are needed for complex hierarchical textures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transposer: Universal Texture Synthesis Using Feature Maps as Transposed Convolution Filter

ideas.title: Apply neural ODEs for continuous texture scale
ideas.description: Model the synthesis process as a continuous flow to enable arbitrary resolution scaling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transposer: Universal Texture Synthesis Using Feature Maps as Transposed Convolution Filter

ideas.title: Lyapunov-like stability criteria for model-data feedback loops
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from control theory. The analogous problem is proving the stability of nonlinear dynamical systems, classically solved using Lyapunov's direct method. The adapted idea is to develop a more rigorous theoretical framework for the stability of model-data feedback loops by constructing a formal, Lyapunov-like stability analysis for the coupled dynamical system formed by a learning model and the data distribution it influences. This involves defining a suitable Lyapunov function on the joint model-data state space and deriving sufficient conditions—potentially related to learning rates, model capacity, and data collection policies—under which this function decays, thereby providing formal, verifiable guarantees of loop stability, convergence, and robustness against pathological oscillations or divergence.

ideas.title: Incorporate temporal or video instructions
ideas.description: Expand the benchmark to handle instructions over video inputs, not just static images.
ideas.sourceTitle: VisIT-Bench: A Benchmark for Vision-Language Instruction Following Inspired by Real-World Use

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartment Models to Forecast Data Distribution Dominance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from epidemiology, specifically compartment models like SIR used to forecast disease spread, to the problem of predicting long-term outcomes in AI feedback systems. In the analogous problem, populations move between states (Susceptible, Infected, Recovered) based on quantifiable transmission and recovery rates. The adapted solution treats competing data distributions as 'populations' in different states within the AI's learning environment. By defining analogous transition rates—such as the 'infection rate' at which a model's output contaminates a clean data source, or the 'recovery rate' at which data curation efforts reintroduce diversity—we can create a predictive mathematical framework. This framework will forecast whether one data distribution will drive others to extinction, or if parameters can be tuned to achieve a sustainable, diverse data ecosystem, directly informing the governance of long-running AI training cycles.

ideas.title: A Social Science of AI Trust: Measuring How Task Design, Not Just Model Flaws, Shapes Perceived Hallucinations and Reliance
ideas.description: This research program applies rigorous social science methods—such as controlled experiments, surveys, and behavioral analysis—to investigate how different task formulations (e.g., open-ended generation vs. retrieval-augmented summarization) systematically influence users' perceptions of AI hallucinations and, consequently, their trust and reliance behaviors. It tests the core hypothesis that user-reported trust breakdowns are often triggered by task-specific interaction patterns rather than inherent model error rates. The outcome will be a predictive, human-factors model of trust calibration that provides actionable guidelines for designing AI tasks and interfaces that proactively manage user expectations and minimize over- or under-reliance.

ideas.title: Causal Identification and Mitigation of Pathological Preference Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to causally disentangle the drivers of human preference distribution shifts, specifically separating changes caused by the AI system's own outputs (endogenous feedback) from broader societal evolution (exogenous drift). It establishes formal criteria for 'pathological' loops, such as those that reduce long-term user welfare or societal robustness, using simulation and controlled experiments. The core output is a suite of online learning algorithms that can adapt to benign exogenous shifts while deploying interventions—like strategic diversification of recommendations or incentive structures—to identify and mitigate harmful endogenous feedback dynamics before they become entrenched.

ideas.title: Adversarial Attack Surface Discovery via Instruction-Image Failure Maximization
ideas.description: This research applies cybersecurity threat modeling principles to the security of multimodal (vision-language) foundation models by automating the discovery of adversarial attack surfaces. The core method involves, for a fixed instruction, algorithmically searching or generating the image that maximizes the model's failure probability, thereby identifying 'hard' semantic intersections between text and image that constitute vulnerabilities. This process systematically maps failure regions in the model's joint embedding space, revealing novel attack vectors beyond simple image perturbations, such as contextual contradictions or deceptive compositions. The resulting catalog of failure-maximizing instruction-image pairs provides a structured threat model for proactive defense, guiding the development of more robust training and evaluation benchmarks.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Human-AI Trust Landscapes
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological framework of Ecological Niche Modeling, specifically Species Distribution Models (SDMs), from ecology to the study of human-AI trust. In ecology, SDMs statistically correlate species occurrence data with environmental layers to predict geographic distributions. Here, we replace the ecological variables with psychosocial and system-performance variables (e.g., explanation quality, error rate, user's prior experience) and replace species occurrence with quantified measures of trust (e.g., reliance rates, survey scores). The solution involves collecting rich interaction data, using machine learning or statistical techniques (like MaxEnt) to model the 'trust niche,' and generating predictive landscapes that identify the combinations of factors most conducive to trust. This provides a rigorous, data-driven model for understanding and forecasting the complex dynamics of trust in human-AI interaction.

ideas.title: Transfer Attacks via Ecological Niche Modeling and Substitution
ideas.description: This idea expands on transfer attacks using model substitution by drawing an analogy from Ecology and Invasive Species Biology. In that field, to manage an invasive species in a hard-to-study target ecosystem, scientists create a predictive model based on the species' behavior in a similar, accessible habitat (its ecological niche). Analogously, for attacking a secured black-box ML model (the target ecosystem), we propose building a highly accurate substitute model not just by querying the target, but by first reverse-engineering its 'decision niche'—the underlying data manifold and feature correlations it likely relies on. This substitute is trained on a proxy dataset engineered to occupy this same niche. Adversarial examples crafted against this niche-accurate substitute model are then more fundamentally aligned with the target's vulnerabilities, significantly improving transfer attack success rates compared to substitutes trained only on architecture mimicry or limited query data.

ideas.title: Multimodal Feedback for Robustness Auditing: Closing the Real-World Gap
ideas.description: This research proposes using large multimodal models (LMMs) not just for scaling, but as automated 'robustness auditors' for traditional vision models. The LMM generates diverse, challenging, and semantically coherent multimodal inputs (e.g., text descriptions of edge cases, corresponding synthetic images, or adversarial code for image generation) to probe a target vision model's performance on real-world tasks. The failures discovered through this automated probing are then used to create targeted corrective feedback, which is fed back into the LMM's own training loop to improve its auditing capabilities. This creates a closed-loop system where the LMM evolves to become increasingly adept at finding and helping to fix the very robustness shortcomings that static datasets like ImageNet fail to capture.

ideas.title: Scale up multi-task with image-only loss
ideas.description: Combine CLIP's contrastive loss with a strong image-only auxiliary task during pre-training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is a Caption Worth a Thousand Images? A Controlled Study for Representation Learning

ideas.title: Cross-Task Knowledge Infusion via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the closed-loop, single-task assumption by examining open-loop feedback where data/representations from one task/model influence training for a different task. The analogy is drawn from ecology, specifically the concept of primary succession, where pioneer species modify an environment to make it suitable for a different, subsequent ecological community. The analogous solution is this directional, stage-wise conditioning process. Adapted to machine learning, we propose treating a model on a source task as a 'pioneer' that transforms raw data into a structured representation 'environment.' This representation then serves as the direct input for training a model on a distinct target task. The research would formalize the principles of this 'knowledge infusion,' analyzing how the pioneer's architecture and training dynamics prepare a representation habitat that facilitates or hinders the successor, moving beyond fine-tuning to study open-loop, cross-task ecological succession in model training.

ideas.title: Causal Mechanistic Regularization: Training Foundation Models with Enforced Interpretable Subroutines
ideas.description: This research develops training-time interventions that enforce the formation of causally interpretable internal mechanisms in foundation models. Instead of analyzing a finished model, it designs loss functions and architectural constraints that reward the use of human-understandable algorithmic sub-circuits (e.g., for logical deduction or feature composition) during the forward pass. The system includes verification protocols to test the causal necessity of these sub-circuits for model outputs, ensuring explanations are not post-hoc rationalizations. This results in models whose core decision processes are inherently more transparent, auditable, and aligned with robust reasoning.

ideas.title: Integrate curriculum learning into training
ideas.description: Structure the simulated feedback tasks by difficulty to create a curriculum for more stable policy learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback

ideas.title: The 'Immune System' Paradigm for Model Adaptation: When Targeted, Localized Responses Outweigh Systemic Retraining
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, this research challenges the assumption that transfer learning (full model fine-tuning) is always the optimal adaptation method. In immunology, the body doesn't rebuild itself for each new pathogen; it uses a general innate system paired with targeted antibodies. The analogous solution is this layered, localized response. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to treat a large pre-trained model as a static, general-purpose 'innate' system. Adaptation to new tasks or domains is achieved not by retraining this core system, but by synthesizing and applying lightweight, specialized components—such as prompts, adapters, or retrieval mechanisms—that act like immunological antibodies. The research will systematically explore whether these fundamentally more localized and contained adaptation paradigms offer superior safety (reducing catastrophic forgetting and preserving broad capabilities), efficiency, and robustness compared to the systemic alteration of full transfer learning.

ideas.title: Applying Clinical Trial Phasing to Web-Scale Pipeline Interventions
ideas.description: This research adapts the phased clinical trial framework from clinical pharmacology to the problem of testing calibration interventions on real-world, web-scale data pipelines. The analogous solution involves a structured, risk-managed progression from small-scale safety tests to large-scale efficacy confirmation. The adapted idea is to design a multi-phase experimental protocol for live systems: starting with a small, isolated data stream to assess stability and computational overhead (Phase A), progressing to a larger, non-critical pipeline to measure the calibration effect on downstream models (Phase B), and finally conducting a controlled, A/B-style deployment on a major production pipeline to confirm results at scale (Phase C). This approach provides a rigorous, ethical, and practical methodology for validating interventions in complex, high-stakes industrial environments without risking system-wide failure or data corruption.

ideas.title: Relax need for large dataset
ideas.description: Develop techniques for language-supervised learning to work well with smaller, curated datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is a Caption Worth a Thousand Images? A Controlled Study for Representation Learning

ideas.title: Constrained Evolutionary Manifolds with Adversarial Specification Auditing
ideas.description: This system treats dataset evolution as navigation within a formally defined manifold, where movement is permitted only within hard constraint boundaries that preserve critical invariants. The target is not a single point but a region satisfying a dynamic objective, which is continually validated by auxiliary models trained to detect specification gaming or distributional shortcuts. A meta-controller adjusts the feedback loops based on this audit, creating a second-order feedback system that ensures the primary evolution remains aligned with the true, often non-stationary, intent behind the target specification.

ideas.title: Invert evaluation: instruction generation from output
ideas.description: Instead of following instructions, task models to infer the instruction given an image and response.
ideas.sourceTitle: VisIT-Bench: A Benchmark for Vision-Language Instruction Following Inspired by Real-World Use

ideas.title: Applying Immune System Memory and Adaptation Mechanisms to AI Robustness
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from immunology, where the adaptive immune system maintains long-term robustness through antigen memory and targeted adaptation, to the problem of AI system robustness against distribution shifts and failures. The analogous immunological solution involves B/T cells storing memories of past pathogens and using somatic hypermutation in germinal centers to generate specific antibodies for novel threats. The adapted idea proposes an AI architecture with two key components: (1) a 'memory module' that maintains a searchable bank of encoded experiences with past anomalies and distribution shifts, and (2) an 'adaptation module' that, upon detection of a novel out-of-distribution input or performance drop, initiates a localized, constrained optimization process. This process would mutate or adjust only a targeted subset of model parameters (inspired by hypermutation) to generate a 'countermeasure' specialized for the new challenge, while preserving the core model knowledge. The system would thus achieve ecological resilience by balancing a stable 'memory' of past environments with a capacity for rapid, targeted adaptation to novel perturbations, preventing catastrophic failures and reducing retraining needs.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Stability Theory to Model Dataset Evolution Under Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using analogical reasoning from ecology to investigate how feedback loops in ML data pipelines affect long-term dataset properties. The analogous problem is understanding ecosystem stability under dynamic interactions and external shocks, which ecologists solve with frameworks centered on resilience, resistance, and regime shifts. The adapted idea is to model a dataset as an evolving ecosystem, defining formal metrics for dataset resilience (e.g., recovery of representational diversity after a filtering feedback loop), resistance (e.g., maintaining factual correctness against systematic error propagation), and thresholds for catastrophic regime shifts (e.g., a permanent collapse into a low-quality, homogeneous state). This moves the analysis beyond static bias audits to a dynamic systems view of dataset health.

ideas.title: Study model failure modes systematically

ideas.title: Quantifying and Controlling Pathological Attractors in Multi-Model AI Feedback Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research develops a formal, simulation-based framework to model multi-model AI ecosystems where model outputs recursively influence future training data. It defines and tracks key metrics for system health, including output diversity, distribution drift, and performance stability. The core of the approach is to identify and characterize pathological attractor states—such as collapse to homogeneous, low-quality, or biased outputs—using tools from dynamical systems. Finally, it designs and tests targeted intervention protocols, like strategic fresh data injection or reward model adjustments, to steer the ecosystem away from these failures and towards robust, high-performing equilibria.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Adaptive Surrogate Ensemble for Robust Query-Efficient Black-Box Attacks
ideas.description: This system overcomes the brittleness of standard Bayesian optimization in non-stationary attack landscapes by employing a meta-learned ensemble of surrogate models. An online meta-controller dynamically selects and combines models from an ensemble based on their recent predictive performance, adapting to local smoothness, noise, and dimensionality. This adaptive surrogate is coupled with a trust region strategy that expands or contracts based on query success, ensuring robust exploration. The result is a more query-efficient black-box attack that maintains high success rates across diverse models and non-convex loss surfaces where vanilla BO fails.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Adversarial Ecosystem for Continuous Evaluation of Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research designs a continuous, adaptive evaluation framework that moves beyond static benchmarks. It establishes a hybrid human-AI red teaming platform to perpetually generate novel, multi-modal challenges targeting known and speculative failure modes. Simultaneously, it develops methods for instrumenting and monitoring models in diverse deployment environments to feed real-world failure data back into the adversarial challenge loop. This creates a living benchmark system that evolves with model capabilities, proactively surfaces emergent risks, and provides a more robust assessment of real-world robustness and limitations.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for Real-Time Adaptive Adversarial Audio Perturbations
ideas.description: This research develops a reinforcement learning agent that generates adversarial perturbations for streaming audio by interacting with a simulated real-time ASR environment. The training process is structured via curriculum learning, where the agent first learns on simplified tasks (e.g., static noise models, predictable speech) and gradually progresses to complex, dynamic scenarios (e.g., variable latency, background noise, speaker changes). This curriculum enables stable and efficient policy learning, allowing the agent to discover sophisticated perturbation strategies that are effective against the temporal and causal constraints of live systems. The result is an adaptive attack model that achieves higher success rates and better transferability to real-world streaming platforms than methods trained on static, offline datasets.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Analysis for Breaking Data-Model Homogenization Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research moves beyond observation to establish causal mechanisms and test interventions. It constructs a simulated ecosystem of competing model developers, where data generation, model performance, and adoption are linked in a closed loop. The system runs counterfactual experiments to isolate how different data regimes causally impact the diversity of resulting architectures and training methods. Finally, it designs and evaluates specific policy and technical interventions within the simulation to identify which are most effective at preventing harmful homogenization and fostering a robustly diverse model ecosystem.

ideas.title: Adversarial Signals Designed for Noisy Channels via Communication Theory
ideas.description: This research challenges the clean, digital-domain assumption by creating adversarial attacks robust to real-world noise and codecs. Drawing an analogy from digital communication theory—where signals are engineered to survive noisy, lossy channels using pre-distortion, error correction, and modulation—we propose to design adversarial perturbations as robust communication signals. The adapted idea is to model the real-world processing pipeline (sensor noise, compression, transmission) as a noisy channel, and then pre-code the adversarial perturbation using techniques inspired by communication systems (e.g., shaping its frequency components, adding structured redundancy) to ensure it remains potent after this distortion, thereby creating attacks effective in physical and processed domains.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Adversarial Policies for Resource-Constrained Physical Agents
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework specifically designed to generate and execute sophisticated adversarial policies on low-resource, embedded hardware typical of physical-world systems like drones or IoT devices. The high-level policy learns abstract, goal-oriented attack strategies (e.g., 'evade detection while approaching target'), which are decomposed by a trained low-level policy into sequences of hardware-efficient primitive actions. This division of labor allows the agent to perform complex, multi-stage adversarial maneuvers that would be infeasible with a monolithic policy on resource-constrained platforms, directly demonstrating the feasibility of adaptive, intelligent threats in the physical domain.

ideas.title: Immunological Audit: Training Specialized Adversarial Models to Probe and Stress-Test Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the immunological solution of using controlled adversarial challenges (like adjuvants and attenuated pathogens) to stress-test and train biological systems. The analogous field is Immunology and Vaccine Development, where agents are designed to probe for weaknesses and enhance defensive robustness without causing catastrophic failure. Adapted to AI research, the proposal is to train a diverse suite of specialized 'adversarial probe' models, each engineered to exploit a specific failure mode in foundation models (e.g., logical inconsistency, harmful bias, prompt injection vulnerability). These probes function not as general critics but as targeted audit tools. They would be deployed in a structured, iterative protocol to systematically audit foundation models, generating high-quality, targeted adversarial examples that expose vulnerabilities. This output can then directly feed into reinforcement learning from adversarial criticism (RLaC) or other safety fine-tuning processes, creating a closed-loop system for identifying and hardening model weaknesses, thereby scaling the dual capability of critique and improvement.

ideas.title: OOD Robustness as a Predictor for Real-World Adversarial Risk
ideas.description: This research investigates whether a model's performance on Out-of-Distribution (OOD) data corrupted by realistic environmental noise and processing artifacts can serve as a reliable predictor for its vulnerability to in-distribution (ID) adversarial attacks. We propose to develop adversarial attacks that are explicitly optimized to be robust to these real-world corruptions, creating a more practical threat model. The core synergy is using the measurable OOD performance drop as a guide to efficiently craft or select adversarial examples that are both effective in the digital domain and likely to transfer through physical-world channels. This enables a novel security audit paradigm: by first benchmarking a model on noisy OOD data, an evaluator can estimate its inherent adversarial robustness and prioritize attack strategies without exhaustive clean-model attacks.

ideas.title: Anchored Domain Specialization: Constraining Deeply Curated Training with Persistent General Knowledge
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to safely specialize foundation models for specific domains without losing essential general-world knowledge. It pairs a frozen, broadly pre-trained model that anchors common sense with a trainable module specialized on deep, curated domain data. The system uses representation alignment and constrained optimization during specialization to prevent catastrophic forgetting in the adaptable component. This enables robust domain expertise while preserving the model's ability to handle out-of-domain queries and integrate cross-disciplinary concepts.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Steering: Using Feedback Loop Dynamics to Evolve Datasets Toward Specified Property Targets
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for 'diagnostic steering' of iterative dataset curation. It first rigorously models how existing feedback loops (e.g., from model-in-the-loop filtering) causally affect a spectrum of dataset properties—including diversity, representational quality, and factual veracity—over time. It then inverts this diagnostic model to design targeted interventions within the feedback loop, transforming observed instability into a controlled dynamical system. The goal is to algorithmically guide the dataset's evolution from its current state toward a user-specified target distribution of these multifaceted properties, ensuring the curation process is both predictable and directed.

ideas.title: Learnable Orchestration for Robust Multi-Model Systems
ideas.description: This research designs systems of specialized, interacting models governed by a central, learnable orchestration engine. The orchestrator is meta-trained to understand the capabilities, failure modes, and costs of each component model, enabling it to dynamically plan and execute complex tasks by composing them. It actively monitors for inconsistencies or errors in the component outputs, implementing verification and fallback strategies to ensure system reliability. This approach preserves specialization while providing the robustness, predictability, and manageability of a unified system.

ideas.title: Data Influence Attribution and Membership Characterization via Counterfactual Model Auditing
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic methodology to audit whether specific, known data samples likely influenced a finalized foundation model's training. Instead of attempting blind reconstruction, the approach trains a suite of controlled counterfactual models with and without candidate data points. It then defines and measures robust statistical signatures of data influence—such as directional derivatives in loss, representation shifts, or prediction changes on carefully designed probe tasks—to characterize a data point's traceable membership and functional impact. This transforms an ill-posed inverse problem into a testable hypothesis framework for data attribution and influence auditing.

ideas.title: Decentralized Curation of Feedback Loops to Diversify Foundation Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research proposes a system where the outputs of a large, foundational model are not directly scraped for future training, but are instead routed through a decentralized network of independent, application-specific curator models. Each curator applies distinct, often competing, values, filters, and objectives to the raw outputs, creating a plurality of curated training corpora. These diverse corpora are then used to fine-tune or continually train a population of specialized downstream models, preventing a monolithic feedback loop and fostering a resilient ecosystem of differentiated models. The core technical challenge is designing incentive and coordination mechanisms for the curator network to ensure the resulting model diversity is functionally beneficial and reduces systemic collapse risk.

ideas.title: Incorporate perceptual masking from visual saliency for imperceptible adversarial perturbations
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from image and video compression, where perceptual models like visual saliency and Just Noticeable Difference (JND) are used to hide compression artifacts in less perceptible regions. The analogous solution allocates distortion to areas where the human visual system is less sensitive. Adapted to adversarial machine learning, this involves using computational models of visual attention and contrast sensitivity to strategically place adversarial perturbations. The perturbations would be optimized to reside primarily in image regions with high natural masking potential (e.g., complex textures, high-contrast edges, or visually non-salient zones), thereby exploiting the human visual system's own limitations to achieve stronger imperceptibility for a given attack strength or norm constraint.

ideas.title: Ecosystem-Aware Hallucination Control: Modeling and Real-Time Mitigation of Hallucination Dynamics in LLMs
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that treats factual and hallucinatory data distributions within a large language model's knowledge base as competing species in an ecological model. By applying population dynamics models (e.g., Lotka-Volterra equations), the system can predict the long-term prevalence and 'fitness' of hallucinatory patterns under different feedback regimes from user interactions and model fine-tuning. These predictive dynamics are then integrated with a low-latency, streaming hallucination detector, allowing the system to not only flag individual hallucinations in real-time but also to strategically prioritize mitigation efforts based on the predicted trajectory of the hallucination 'species'. This enables proactive, ecosystem-level management of model output quality, moving beyond reactive correction to actively shaping the internal data distribution landscape for long-term reliability.

ideas.title: Controlled Multimodal Feedback with Auditor-Guided Synthetic Data Curation
ideas.description: This research investigates safe feedback mechanisms for large multimodal models by designing a closed-loop system with a mandatory curation layer. The core innovation is a parallel ecosystem of smaller, specialized auditor models and human reviewers that critically assess all AI-generated text, code, and images before any are considered for training. The system dynamically adjusts data admission thresholds based on quality metrics and distributional shifts, actively preventing contamination and collapse. The outcome is a framework for sustainable self-improvement that prioritizes data integrity and long-term stability over raw scale.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Theory to Model Development Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research applies the ecological framework of succession and the intermediate disturbance hypothesis to the problem of feedback loops reducing architectural diversity in AI. The analogous problem in ecology is the loss of biodiversity when a single species dominates an ecosystem, often solved through managed interventions that reset competitive hierarchies. The adapted idea is to analyze how data feedback loops act as a selective pressure, leading to a monoculture of model architectures. The solution is to experimentally design and simulate 'managed disturbances' within the AI development pipeline—inspired by ecological conservation tactics—to see which interventions (e.g., periodic benchmark retirement, diversity-promoting grants, or data diet variations) most effectively sustain a diverse and resilient ecosystem of machine learning models and training methodologies.

ideas.title: Mitigate Homogenization via Portfolio Diversification for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the principle of portfolio diversification from financial risk management to address the systemic risk of homogenization when many applications rely on adaptations of a single base foundation model. In finance, diversifying investments across uncorrelated assets mitigates the impact of any one asset's failure. Analogously, we propose developing techniques to create a 'model portfolio'—a curated set of adapted models fine-tuned with different objectives, data subsets, or architectural interventions to maximize functional diversity and minimize correlated errors for a target application space. A meta-model or orchestration system would then manage this portfolio, potentially blending outputs or selecting the most appropriate model(s) per query, thereby distributing risk and increasing the overall robustness of the system against shared vulnerabilities inherent in the common base model.

ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Chain-of-Custody Protocols for Model Lineage Provenance
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring legal discovery tools to audit AI models. By analogy, the field of digital forensics has a solved parallel problem: ensuring the integrity and provenance of digital evidence for legal proceedings through chain-of-custody protocols. The analogous solution uses cryptographic hashing, secure logging, and tamper-evident documentation at every handling step. Adapted to the research context, this yields a method for creating a forensic chain-of-custody for model lineage. The adapted idea is to implement a system that applies these protocols to document the complete lifecycle of a model's training data and decisions. This involves generating immutable, timestamped hashes for datasets and model states at each critical juncture, creating a verifiable audit trail that can withstand rigorous scrutiny, similar to evidence presented in a legal discovery process.

ideas.title: Contrastive Feedback Loops: Analyzing Data-Driven Architectural Homogenization via Hard Negative Mining
ideas.description: This research investigates the inverse relationship between data and model diversity by leveraging the mechanics of contrastive metric learning. It proposes using hard negative mining within a CLIP-like framework to construct a 'diversity sensitivity metric' that quantifies how different data distributions and feedback loops pull model representations into homogenized basins of attraction. By simulating training cycles with progressively filtered or synthetic data, the study would measure the contraction of the viable architectural and loss-function space, predicting which model designs become 'trapped' by the data ecology. This provides a predictive, contrastive-based tool for understanding and potentially mitigating the architectural monoculture induced by large-scale data feedback loops.
